Analyst (Ajox Frederick) asked:
Okay. And then secondly on the guidance, right, we have a very strong guidance on
powertrain, aluminum and even storage business. So, if I tie it up to the underlying be
it CV or PV industry, we may not see such high-volume growth. So, where is this
confidence coming for us to deliver 20%-plus kind of growth in an organic manner?

Management answered:
We have been diligently trying to diversify our customer portfolio. Earlier, we had six
customers giving 50% of our revenue and balance 50% of customers coming from equal
100 customers I would say. Now, the top 60% revenue is coming from 12 customers
and in diversified end customer segments. So, our product mix is like this and the
company side revenue, I mean consolidated if we look at it, the commercial vehicle
portion is coming to 17%, two wheeler is coming to 18%, passenger vehicle is 32%,
storage is 10%, off-highways is 5% and tractor is 4%. I think overall we have diversified
quite well and we have also invested for customers who are export-oriented. Many of
the OEMs from the eastern part of the world like Japan and Korea are also setting their
sights on African market for both passenger car, two-wheeler, pickup trucks, tractors
and all the products which are going to go into Africa in the future, which is the last
market to be developed, the products are similar to India, what India wanted in the last
two decades. But India is migrating to slightly higher products now. So, these products
Page 9 of 11

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Thanks for the opportunity. Sir, my question is on the guidance. In last quarter you have
guided around Rs.7,000 crores of revenue and EBITDA would be around Rs.1,100
crores and EBIT would be around Rs.700 crores. Do you maintain that guidance in this?

Management answered:
Yes.

Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Got it, sir. You mentioned in past the margin for the segment can be very good for the
heavy engines part. I just want to understand, I mean this will be more-accretive than
the current margins or how do you see the range of the margin for this business, sir?

Management answered:
See, the current business, you may be aware that we are not backward integrated, and
we have excellent top notch foundry partners who are leaders in India and also the
OEMs themselves, three of the OEMs in India, major OEM's are running their own
foundries where we are getting the casting from. So, it's a mixture of job work and also
we buy the castings from our foundry partners. So, it is an assorted margin what you're
seeing on the current powertrain business whereas the new powertrain business for the
larger engines, yes, it will start also with the sort of job work activity, but the castings
are not being produced in India, 95% of them, there is only one or two small suppliers
here. So, we are getting the castings from Europe and US to start with for the activity
in 2027, while we ramp up our foundry capacity here with the prove out also, the
castings must be validated. So, when we are supplying this material, it looks like as if
the margins will be lower than the conventional powertrain business, but I can assure
you that the margins are in line with the current powertrain business as in formula wise
how we calculate. When we are buying the castings from elsewhere there cannot be a
margin on the castings, but we make our castings, it will be there, but the margin on the
castings surely it will be slightly lower than the machining because machining is a value
addition, there is no material cost involved. So, it optically look like a higher margin. So,
on the blended average we are on par with whatever we are currently doing.

Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Got it, sir. Good to see the improvement in the standalone margin. Sir, particularly in
the powertrain segment we have seen improvement this quarter. What led to the
improvement there? And also can you update how the Kothavadi plant order book is
shaping up for the heavy engines and for the FY26 and '27, how do you see the revenue
trends there?

Management answered:
Powertrain in the beginning, except the last quarter, I think for the last maybe almost
18 months, we went through quite a modernization program, repair and maintenance,
which was hitting the expenses I would say, that is one thing. The second thing is the
operating leverage was quite less, capacity utilization was quite less across the old
lines. New lines also were not ramping up. The customers were slow. So, now overall
slight improvement in the operating leverage has happened. That is helping us totally.
There has been from the multinational companies who set up plants in India they have
started to produce with the one- or two-years delay, I would say. And there is some
slight revival in the farm sector and a slight revival in the commercial vehicle sector and
whatever is the downside we had experiencing in the many quarters that has more or
less stabilized now, so that nothing more and whatever we are able to utilize the plants
slightly better and optimize our cost. These margins will be sustained or improved
depending on the quarter of the next financial year, but for the full financial year, we can
expect a better result on the powertrain, i.e., accumulative result even when compared
to the Q4 result. So, the margins will creep up just like the period before we did this
massive correction on the powertrain. So, any questions on the fundamental powertrain
business, then I will come to the new powertrain business.

results.question: What was the impact of raw material price volatility, and what inventory loss/gain was reported?
results.expected_answer: Sandeep Engineer (Q2 FY25 call): 'PVC prices went down by 13.5% during a single quarter... from beginning of November the prices have started an uptrend... due to this antidumping duty in place, the PVC prices went up by INR1 on 1st of November and again by INR2 on 7th of November.' Hiranand Savlani (Q3 FY25 call, on the same anti-dumping duty context): 'Already INR3 price rise is taken place. We are expecting between INR6 to INR8 price rise should happen over a period of time.' Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet, FY25 annual): 'last year alone, the polymer price was down by 18%. Despite a 18% fall... Astral is the only company in the country that has improved its gross profit margin. We have improved the GP by 1%.' Inventory loss/gain impact was also quantified: Hiranand Savlani (Q2 call): 'a slight loss of inventory also in this quarter... between INR10 crores to INR15 crores kind of loss.'
results.answer:
## Details

- Raw Material Cost and Change in Inventory figures from Excel profit & loss data for FY2024 and FY2025:

  - FY2024:
    - Raw Material Cost: ₹2521.53 Cr
    - Change in Inventory: ₹150.22 Cr (positive)
  
  - FY2025:
    - Raw Material Cost: ₹3296.73 Cr
    - Change in Inventory: ₹194.66 Cr (positive)

- From Annual Report 2024-25 (PDF text):

  - Inventory valuation policy states that if there is objective evidence of diminution in value of inventories (due to obsolescence, damage, market conditions, or significant declines in selling prices), the carrying amount is reduced to net realizable value.
  
  - No explicit mention of inventory loss or write-down amount is found in the retrieved context.
  
  - The Annual Report mentions sustainable sourcing to minimize risks related to material price volatility and supply chain disruptions (Page 70, FY25), indicating management awareness of raw material price volatility risk.
  
  - Hedge accounting notes (Pages 119, 124, 125, 143) mention that gains or losses on hedging instruments related to commodity price risk are recognized in Other Income or Other Comprehensive Income, but no explicit figures for gains or losses from raw material price volatility or inventory valuation adjustments are provided.

- No explicit figure for inventory loss or gain due to raw material price volatility or inventory write-down is disclosed in the retrieved context.

## Summary

The company recognizes the risk of raw material price volatility and manages it through sustainable sourcing and hedging practices, as noted in the Annual Report. Inventory valuation follows a policy to write down inventories to net realizable value if there is evidence of diminution, but no explicit inventory loss or gain amount related to raw material price volatility or inventory write-downs is reported in the retrieved context. The positive change in inventory values in FY2024 and FY2025 (₹150.22 Cr and ₹194.66 Cr respectively) indicates inventory accumulation but does not specify any loss or gain from valuation adjustments.

results.question: What are the capital expenditure (capex) plans and guidance for the current and next fiscal year?
results.expected_answer: Hiranand Savlani (Q3 FY25 call, in response to Rahul Agarwal): 'I think we should be closing around Rs. 450 crore or so for the full year' (FY25) and for the following year, 'Next year should be somewhere around 250 or so' (FY26), explained by Sandeep Engineer as being lower because 'Kanpur is almost completed' and no major new facility additions are planned, with spending mainly on machinery rather than land/buildings. In the Q2 FY25 call, capex for H1 FY25 was stated as INR282 crores with full-year capex expected to be 'the same line only... now balance will be more than INR100 crores' with Kanpur cited as a driver. In the Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani separately noted 'In the last two years alone, we have pumped in close to ₹1000 crore into this expansion activity, particularly capex,' with FY26 capex guided at '250 to 300 crore max because Kanpur is still yet to be completed.'
results.answer:
## Details

- From the earnings call transcript (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 5 and 6 · FY25), management stated the following about CAPEX guidance:
  - Total group CAPEX guidance for the current fiscal year is around Rs. 750 crores to Rs. 800 crores.
  - For Craftsman standalone, the expected CAPEX is about Rs. 550 crores.
  - For subsidiaries Sunbeam and DR Axion, CAPEX will be decided as it comes.
  - There is an additional potential Rs. 40-50 crores for repair and maintenance of old equipment at Fronberg, which is under evaluation.

- Management also mentioned that the cash generation run rate will be quite high in the next financial year, supported by a possible land sale at Sunbeam expected in Q3 or Q4, which could add around Rs. 300 crores. However, no explicit CAPEX guidance for the next fiscal year was provided.

## Summary

The capital expenditure guidance for the current fiscal year is Rs. 750-800 crores for the group, with Rs. 550 crores allocated to Craftsman standalone and the remainder to subsidiaries and maintenance. No specific CAPEX guidance for the next fiscal year was disclosed, though management indicated strong cash generation and potential land sale proceeds that could support future investments.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Pages 5-7 · FY25

results.question: What capacity additions or plant updates did management detail?
results.expected_answer: Specific figures given in the Q3 FY25 call (Hiranand Savlani, in response to Keshav Lahoti): 'This year we have already added 47,000 metric tons in 9 month basis' of pipe capacity; Kanpur plant total capacity will be '60,000' metric tons 'in two phases. First will be 30 and then the second will be another 30' with '30 will be coming to FY26 and 30 will be coming to FY27'; Hyderabad plant total planned capacity was '70,000,' of which 'we already completed I think 25,000 metric tonne.' Separately, in the Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani stated capacity has grown at a '10.3% CAGR, while sales are happening at a 13.5% CAGR,' with utilization 'in the 55 to 65 range.' In the Q3 call it was also stated overall capacity increased 'by 36,000 metric tons from 334k to 370k metric tons' during the year.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript (crafts25Q4.pdf):

- Management mentioned that in the aluminum segment, the current utilization is around 70-75%, with a strong order book.
- They plan to increase CAPEX to quickly reach 80-85% utilization by Q2FY25.
- By Q3FY25, further CAPEX will be needed for new orders already in the pipeline.
- For the conventional powertrain business, utilization is around 70%, with an upper limit of 85-90% due to seasonality and market factors.
- No explicit mention of new plant constructions or expansions beyond CAPEX for capacity increase.
- Additionally, alloy wheel and other parts previously imported from China are now being localized in India, indicating a shift in sourcing but not necessarily new capacity addition.

## Summary

Management detailed capacity additions primarily through planned CAPEX investments aimed at increasing utilization in the aluminum segment from about 70-75% to 80-85% by Q2FY25, with further CAPEX planned by Q3FY25 for new orders. For the conventional powertrain segment, utilization is around 70% with some headroom up to 85-90%. They also noted localization of alloy wheel imports from China to India but did not specify new plant constructions.

results.question: What updates did management provide regarding new acquisitions, overseas offices, or capacity expansion?
results.expected_answer: Management discussed the acquisition of Al Aziz (Sandeep Engineer, Analyst Meet): rationale was to enter the PE/PP pipe and fittings product line, including German-approved gas fittings and water-line fittings; 'Now we are getting four machines—three are coming, and one more will come later—for making PE and PP pipes in our plants.' No specific investment amount or timeline in months/years was disclosed for this acquisition. The company also opened its 'first overseas office in Dubai' (Hiranand Savlani, Analyst Meet) targeting Middle East/Gulf/Africa exports - no investment figure given. On new geography for Paint, Sandeep Engineer/Hiranand Savlani (Q2 FY25 call) discussed phased launches: Astral Paint launched in Gujarat, then Rajasthan/Jaipur, with plans to enter 'Maharashtra and MP in next 5 to 6 months' - qualitative timeline given but no specific investment figure. UK adhesive technology/backward integration deal was also mentioned (Hiranand Savlani, Q3 call): 'we have just signed the agreement. So in the Q1, that will be available to us' - no investment amount disclosed.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report text (Annual-Report-2025.pdf):

- The company made acquisitions during the year:
  - Acquired the remaining 24% equity shares of DR Axion India Private Limited on 29th June 2024, making DR Axion a wholly owned subsidiary effective 1st July 2024.
  - Acquired 100% equity stake of Craftsman Germany GmbH, Germany, thereby acquiring its subsidiary Craftsman Fronberg Guss GmbH, Germany on 22nd July 2024.

- Regarding overseas offices, the acquisition of Craftsman Germany GmbH and its subsidiary indicates expansion of overseas presence in Germany.

- No explicit mention of capacity expansion or new capacity additions was found in the retrieved context.

- No management commentary from earnings call transcripts or other sources specifically mentioned capacity expansion or new overseas offices beyond the acquisitions noted.

## Summary

Management updated that during FY25, the company acquired the remaining stake in DR Axion India Private Limited, making it a wholly owned subsidiary, and acquired Craftsman Germany GmbH along with its subsidiary, expanding its overseas presence in Germany. There was no explicit update on capacity expansion or new overseas offices beyond these acquisitions in the retrieved context.

results.question: What EBITDA margin contractions or fluctuations did management report, and what were the reasons?
results.expected_answer: Quantified margin impacts disclosed in the Q2 FY25 call (Hiranand Savlani): consolidated EBITDA fell from 17% (last year) to '15.97%, so precisely 16%,' described as 'a 1% drop on the EBITDA'; consolidated EBITDA in absolute terms fell from 'INR233 crores versus this year, INR219 crores, so almost 6% negative EBITDA growth'; UK adhesive EBITDA swung from '9.7% EBITDA' last year to '2% EBITDA' this quarter, and in a later reference from 'INR9.1 crores' to 'a negative EBITDA of minus INR2 crores'; polymer/PVC price fell 'by almost 13.5%' during the quarter; inventory loss was quantified at 'between INR10 crores to INR15 crores.' In the Q3 FY25 call, pipe EBITDA was maintained/grown, with Sandeep Engineer citing 10.9% EBITDA growth for the pipe vertical despite polymer price volatility, and Analyst Meet figures show GP margin improved 'by 1%' despite an 18% polymer price fall for FY25.
results.answer:
## Details

The provided context includes the EBITDA margin calculation for FY2026:

- EBITDA = 1114.16 (from profit_loss, FY2026)
- Sales = 8069.27 (from profit_loss, FY2026)

Using the formula for EBITDA margin:  
(EBITDA / Sales) * 100 = (1114.16 / 8069.27) * 100 ≈ 13.81%

However, the context does not include any management commentary or reported reasons regarding EBITDA margin contractions or fluctuations.

## Summary

The EBITDA margin for FY2026 is approximately 13.81%, but there is no information provided on management's reported EBITDA margin contractions, fluctuations, or the reasons behind them.

results.question: What is management's stance on debt reduction, cash utilization, and working capital normalization?
results.expected_answer: No specific debt reduction target was disclosed in any of the three transcripts; Astral is described as net cash positive rather than carrying debt to reduce. Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet): 'Right now, we are sitting on a net cash of ₹464 crore.' On cash utilization/dividend, in the Q2 FY25 call, Pinaki Banerjee asked how the company plans to utilize its cash of 'around Rs. 290 crores'; Hiranand Savlani responded: 'I think by year end we will be able to know what is our next year budget. Based on that we will work out our cash utilization plan and if we don't have any such kind of utilization plan then we will give it to our shareholders' - no specific dividend payout ratio or percentage was committed. On working capital, Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet) noted creditor/debtor/inventory day movements tied to a one-off rupee-appreciation-driven early creditor payoff in March, but gave no specific working-capital-day normalization target or timeline in days.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript (crafts25Q4.pdf):

- On working capital accounting: Management clarified that a certain amount is not accounted in working capital but is still lying as cash (Page 10).
- On debt and provisioning: Management explained that Rs.160 crores provisioning was taken at the opening balance when they took over the company. They emphasized that no payment was made for the company shares (shares bought at Re.1), and the money infused went into settling vendor liabilities and some bank liabilities left behind by previous owners. The bank liabilities funded were in lieu of the Gurgaon land sale (Page 8).
- On guidance and outlook: Management maintained their revenue and profitability guidance, implying confidence in operational normalization including working capital (Page 7).

## Summary

Management’s stance is that they have not accounted certain cash as working capital but it remains as cash on the books. They have proactively reduced debt by settling bank liabilities using infused funds rather than share purchase payments. This reflects a focus on debt reduction and cash preservation. Working capital normalization is implied by management’s clarification and maintained financial guidance, indicating confidence in improving working capital and cash utilization.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Pages 7, 8, 10 · FY25  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 131 · FY25

Thank you.

Analyst (Ajox Frederick) asked:
Okay. But it has been accounted in working capital for us, particularly this year?

Management answered:
No, no, that is not accounted. That is still lying as cash.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Thanks for the opportunity. Sir, my question is on the guidance. In last quarter you have
guided around Rs.7,000 crores of revenue and EBITDA would be around Rs.1,100
crores and EBIT would be around Rs.700 crores. Do you maintain that guidance in this?

Management answered:
Yes.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
We have already taken Rs.160 crores provisioning in Quarter 3 or Quarter 4?

Management answered:
At opening balance itself, when you took over the company. What we need to
understand is, we never paid anything for the company per se, it was infused into the
company, it is not for buying any share. Shares are bought at Re.1. That is it. So, all
the money what we have put into the company has gone into the company for the
settlement of maybe some vendors and things like that. Even most of the liability of the
banks were settled by the erstwhile owners and some of the bank liabilities was left
behind which we funded was in lieu of the land sale of Gurgaon.

results.question: What are the reportable business segments of the group, and what was their segment revenue for the current vs previous year?
results.expected_answer: Astral Limited (Group) has two reportable business segments — 'Plumbing' and 'Paints and Adhesives'. Segment revenue for FY25 (year ended March 31, 2025) vs FY24: Plumbing — ₹41,963 Million (FY24: ₹41,420 Million); Paints and Adhesives — ₹16,361 Million (FY24: ₹14,994 Million); Total segment revenue — ₹58,324 Million (FY24: ₹56,414 Million). Note: this segment disclosure is presented only in the Consolidated Financial Statements; the Standalone Financial Statements (Note 41) state that no segment disclosure is given standalone since it is presented in the consolidated statements (Ind AS 108).

results.question: What key business risks does the company identify in its Risk Management framework, and what are their mitigation measures?
results.expected_answer: Not disclosed as a single itemized list in that exact section beyond the categories shown in the 'Risk Management' framework: Market Competition; Technological Relevance; Environmental and Social Responsibility; Talent Acquisition and Retention; Supply Chain Continuity; Product Quality and Safety; Intellectual Property Protection; Compliance and Regulatory Adherence. For each, a corresponding mitigation measure is listed (e.g., for 'Market Competition': advance innovation/enhance product offerings, strengthen distribution reach, prioritise operational efficiency through cost management).
results.answer:
## Details

The company identifies several key business risks in its Risk Management framework, along with corresponding mitigation measures, as detailed in the Annual Report FY25:

1. **Product Safety Risk**  
   - **Risk:** Product defects can lead to significant recalls, damage to brand equity, customer attrition, and regulatory penalties.  
   - **Mitigation:** Maintain product safety and standards to uphold customer trust and competitiveness. Conduct regular safety audits, heavy incident investigations, trainings, risk assessments, provide PPE, safety training, emergency response plans, and obtain certifications like ISO 45001 for Occupational Health and Safety.

2. **Product Design & Lifecycle Opportunity**  
   - **Opportunity:** Integrate sustainability into product design to reduce environmental footprint, open new markets, and improve customer loyalty.  
   - **Mitigation:** Focus on products with better recyclability, lower emissions, and efficient end-of-life management to meet increasing regulations and customer expectations.

3. **Corporate Governance & Risk Management**  
   - **Risk:** Weak governance structures or inadequate risk management can expose the company to compliance violations, financial misstatements, and loss of investor confidence.  
   - **Mitigation:** Implement sound governance practices crucial for long-term resilience.

4. **Occupational Health and Safety Risk**  
   - **Risk:** Health and safety issues in manufacturing, such as accidents, exposure to hazardous materials, ergonomic issues, and regulatory sanctions.  
   - **Mitigation:** Conduct risk assessments, provide safety training, enforce safety protocols, and maintain compliance with local regulations.

5. **Climate Risk and Change**  
   - **Risk:** Physical risks from extreme weather events affecting manufacturing infrastructure, supply chains, and employee safety.  
   - **Opportunity:** Transition to a low-carbon economy by innovating cleaner technologies, conserving energy and resources, lowering operational costs, and accessing green and sustainability funds.  
   - **Mitigation:** Conduct energy audits and adopt sustainable practices.

6. **Competition Risk**  
   - **Risk:** Operating in competitive environments with customers maintaining multiple sources for products/services.  
   - **Mitigation:** Senior management closely monitors the market and devises strategies to stay ahead of competition.

7. **Economic Risk**  
   - **Risk:** Challenging global economic environment with trade tensions, protectionism, slowdown, fiscal profligacy, implementation delays, and weak financial sector.  
   - **Mitigation:** Diversify products, services, markets, and customer base to reduce cyclicality exposure.

8. **Automobile Industry Cyclicality**  
   - **Risk:** Demand vulnerability to interest rates and liquidity in the automobile sector.  
   - **Mitigation:** Increase share of Industrial & Engineering segment to reduce dependence on automobile cyclicality.

9. **Supply Chain Risk**  
   - **Risk:** Risks associated with supply chain disruptions and ESG compliance of suppliers.  
   - **Mitigation:** Develop Supplier Code of Conduct emphasizing ESG criteria, conduct supplier ESG assessments, prefer sourcing from local, certified, or low-carbon footprint vendors, track and monitor supplier ESG KPIs, and maintain transparency and ethics policies.

10. **Market Risk (Foreign Currency and Interest Rate)**  
    - **Risk:** Exposure to changes in foreign currency exchange rates and interest rates affecting fair values or future cash flows.  
    - **Mitigation:** Active management of currency exposures through treasury division and use of derivative financial instruments approved by the Board of Directors.

## Summary

The company identifies key risks including product safety, governance, occupational health and safety, climate change, competition, economic environment, automobile industry cyclicality, supply chain, and market risks (foreign currency and interest rates). Mitigation measures include robust safety protocols, governance frameworks, diversification strategies, supplier ESG management, and active financial risk hedging using derivatives.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 6-7,  67-70,  156 · FY25

results.question: What transactions with related parties were disclosed in the financial statements, and what was the compensation of key management personnel?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 37 (Related Party Disclosures) discloses transactions with Subsidiaries, Joint Venture, Enterprises over which KMP have significant influence, KMP, and Relatives of KMP for FY25 vs FY24 (₹ in Million), including: CSR Expenditure – Astral Foundation 126 (108); Interest Income – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 43 (19); Investment in Subsidiaries – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (2,773); Redemption of debentures – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (1,940); Purchase of Goods/Services – Kairamya Journeys LLP 141 (130); Reimbursement of expenses claimed – Astral Coatings Private Limited 60 (49); Remuneration – Sandeep Engineer 152 (139), Hiranand Savlani 59 (42), Others 57 (52); Rent Paid – Jagruti Sandeep Engineer 1 (1), Sandeep Engineer HUF 1 (1); Sale of Goods – Astral Coatings Private Limited 19 (19); Sitting Fees to Independent Directors (0–1 each); Loans Given – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 477 (91); Loans Repaid – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (8); year-end balances: Loans Given outstanding – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 876 (377); Interest accrued on loan – 20 (5); Payables – Sandeep Engineer 47 (45), Others 5 (4); Advance for purchase of non-current investment – Astral Pipes Limited 109 (109). Compensation of Key Management Personnel (short-term benefits): ₹251 Million (FY24: ₹216 Million).
results.answer:
## Details

- According to the Annual Report FY25 (pages 8, 164, 233), the Company has stated that it **has not entered into any material related party transactions** during the financial year that would require shareholders’ approval under Listing Regulations or disclosure under Section 188 of the Companies Act, 2013. It also confirms that there were **no transactions not at arm’s length**.

- The related party transactions summary (Annual Report FY25, page 164 and 233) shows some transactions with related parties and key management personnel (KMP), including:
  - Purchase of Goods & Services: ₹4 lakhs (FY24-25)
  - Sale of Goods & Services: ₹19.30 lakhs (FY24-25)
  - Reimbursement of Expenditure to related parties: ₹39 lakhs (FY24-25)
  - Reimbursement of Expenditure from related parties: ₹36 lakhs (FY24-25)
  - Interest Income: ₹34 lakhs (FY24-25)
  - Rental Income: ₹6 lakhs (FY24-25)
  - Management Services Income: ₹100 lakhs (₹1 crore) (FY24-25)
  - Commission and Sitting Fees paid to Directors and KMP (detailed on page 233)

- Regarding **compensation of key management personnel (KMP)** (Annual Report FY25, pages 10, 164, 233):
  - The KMP are listed as:
    - Mr. Srinivasan Ravi, Chairman and Managing Director
    - Mr. Ravi Gauthamram, Whole Time Director
    - Mr. C.B. Chandrasekar, Chief Financial Officer
    - Mr. Thiyagaraj Damodharaswamy, Chief Operating Officer – Powertrain
    - Mr. Shainshad Aduvanni, Company Secretary
  - The remuneration paid to KMP during FY24-25 includes:
    - Remuneration to key management personnel: ₹501 lakhs (₹5.01 crore)
    - Commission to Executive Directors: ₹5,916 lakhs (₹59.16 crore)
    - Commission to Non-Executive Directors: ₹5,597 lakhs (₹55.97 crore)
    - Sitting Fees to Non-Executive Directors: ₹149 lakhs (₹1.49 crore)
  - Note: The remuneration to KMP excludes gratuity contributions and is net of amounts paid by the Company and subsidiary.

## Summary

The Company disclosed that there were no material related party transactions requiring shareholder approval or special disclosure under Section 188, and no transactions not at arm’s length during FY25. However, routine related party transactions such as purchase and sale of goods and services, reimbursements, interest and rental income, and management services income were recorded in small amounts. The total remuneration paid to key management personnel was ₹501 lakhs, with significant commission payments to executive and non-executive directors amounting to ₹59.16 crore and ₹55.97 crore respectively, along with sitting fees of ₹1.49 crore.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 8, 10,  164,  233 · FY25

results.question: What operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions did the company detail in the annual report?
results.expected_answer: Directors' Report states that during FY25 the Company increased its installed capacity of the plumbing business by 14.34%, from 3,34,040 MT to 3,81,957 MT, with sales volume rising from 2,19,590 MT to 2,27,090 MT, and incurred capital expenditure of ₹6,177 Million towards plant & machinery, factory building, and other capex. The Letter from the Whole Time Director and CFO (Strategic Review) states nearly ₹1,000 crore was invested in new plants and machinery over the past two years, focused on decentralising manufacturing, and that planned capital expenditure of around ₹300 crore is budgeted for FY26, primarily to complete the Kanpur facility and add further adhesive lines at Dahej. Additionally, the Board approved (April 17, 2025) the acquisition of 100% equity of Al-Aziz Plastics Private Limited (completed May 5, 2025), as part of the strategy to expand product portfolio and enter new high-growth market segments.
results.answer:
## Details

The retrieved context from the annual report and notes does not explicitly mention any specific operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions undertaken by the company during the year ended March 31, 2025. 

- The notes to financial statements (Pages 150, 217) provide details on depreciation, amortization, impairment, and other expenses but do not disclose new capital expenditure projects or expansions.
- The narrative sections and strategy highlights (Page 18) mention broad strategic goals such as maintaining EBITDA margins, improving profitability in certain business segments, diversifying revenue streams, and broadening the business base through strategic diversification initiatives. However, these are stated as strategic objectives rather than specific completed or ongoing expansions or acquisitions.
- The key audit matters (Page 108) discuss accounting for property, plant, and equipment and capital work in progress but do not detail new capacity expansions or acquisitions.
- Subsidiary financial information (Page 179) notes the inclusion of two subsidiaries and their financials but does not describe any new acquisitions or expansions.
- There is no mention of capital expenditure amounts or descriptions of new plants, machinery, or acquisitions in the provided pages.

## Summary

The annual report context provided does not disclose any specific operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions completed or underway during the year. Only broad strategic goals related to growth and diversification are mentioned without detailed project or transaction disclosures.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 18, 108, 150, 179, 217 · FY25

results.question: What Key Audit Matters (KAM) did the independent auditor identify, and what was the auditor's response to them?
results.expected_answer: The Independent Auditor's Report (Standalone) identifies two Key Audit Matters: (1) Impairment assessment of Goodwill (Note 2(x)(iv)) — Goodwill of ₹2,036 Million as at March 31, 2025, tested annually via discounted cash-flow models of Cash Generating Units (CGUs); flagged as a KAM due to judgmental assumptions on projected revenue growth, operating margins, cash flows, and discount rates. Auditor's response: assessed CGU definitions for compliance, evaluated management's future cash flow forecasts, compared forecasts to actual results, focused on sales growth/discount rate assumptions, recalculated estimates, involved valuation specialists, and assessed disclosures. (2) Impairment assessment of investments in subsidiaries (Note 2(x)(iii)) — investments in Astral Coatings Private Limited and Seal IT Services Limited, UK amounting to ₹3,707 Million as at March 31, 2025; flagged as KAM due to judgment in determining value-in-use dependent on management estimates of future performance and cash flows. Auditor's response: evaluated future cash flow forecasts used to compute recoverable amount, compared to business plan/actual results, focused on sales growth/discount rate assumptions, recalculated estimates, involved valuation specialists, and assessed disclosures.
results.answer:
## Details

The independent auditor identified the following Key Audit Matters (KAM) for the current period audit of the standalone and consolidated financial statements of Craftsman Automation Limited:

1. **Accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE)**  
   - The capitalisation of PPE includes the fair value of the assets acquired (including rebates) and any directly attributable costs of bringing the asset to working condition, excluding non-refundable purchase taxes and duties.  
   - Depreciation is charged as per Schedule II to the Companies Act, 2013.  
   - The audit procedures included:  
     - Testing controls and operating effectiveness of internal controls over PPE.  
     - Verifying the capitalisation costs including self-constructed PPE.  
     - Assessing depreciation rates applied.  
     - Reviewing management’s impairment assessment and valuation judgments.  
     - Verifying additions with underlying documentation to assess compliance with accounting standards.

2. **Accounting for Business Combinations**  
   - The audit focused on:  
     - Assessing terms of acquisition agreements and verifying acquisition dates.  
     - Reviewing calculation of total purchase consideration and evaluating fair value measurements of identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed.  
     - Assessing methodologies and assumptions used in valuation.  
     - Verifying computation of goodwill or gain on bargain purchase and reasonableness of underlying assumptions.  
     - Reviewing disclosures in consolidated financial statements for compliance with Ind AS 103 (Business Combinations).

3. **Other Matters**  
   - The auditor also reported on provisions for material foreseeable losses on long-term contracts including derivatives, disclosure of pending litigations, and compliance with transfer of amounts to Investor Education and Protection Fund, as per applicable laws and accounting standards.

The auditor’s response to these KAMs involved detailed substantive procedures and tests of controls, including verification of documentation, assessment of management’s judgments and estimates, and review of compliance with applicable accounting standards and laws.

The auditor concluded that sufficient and appropriate audit evidence was obtained to address these matters and that the standalone and consolidated financial statements give a true and fair view in accordance with Indian Accounting Standards and the Companies Act, 2013.

## Summary

The independent auditor identified Key Audit Matters related to the accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment and Business Combinations, focusing on valuation, capitalisation, depreciation, acquisition accounting, and related disclosures. The auditor responded by performing detailed audit procedures including control testing, substantive verification, and assessment of management’s estimates, concluding that the financial statements present a true and fair view in compliance with applicable standards.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 108-111, 175-182 · FY25

results.question: What segment profit, segment assets, and segment liabilities were reported for the company's core segments?
results.expected_answer: Consolidated Financial Statements, Note 37 (Segment Reporting) — Segment Profit for year ended March 31, 2025 (FY24 in brackets): Plumbing ₹6,126 Million (₹6,054 Million); Paints and Adhesives ₹1,150 Million (₹1,396 Million); Total ₹7,276 Million (₹7,450 Million). Segment Assets as at March 31, 2025 (March 31, 2024): Plumbing ₹28,677 Million (₹24,623 Million); Paints and Adhesives ₹15,603 Million (₹14,222 Million); Total Segment Assets ₹44,280 Million (₹38,845 Million). Segment Liabilities as at March 31, 2025 (March 31, 2024): Plumbing ₹7,521 Million (₹7,657 Million); Paints and Adhesives ₹3,432 Million (₹2,606 Million); Total Segment Liabilities ₹10,953 Million (₹10,263 Million). Note: The report does not disclose a separately labelled 'segment-wise capital employed' figure; only segment assets and segment liabilities are given.
results.answer:
## Details

From the standalone segment data for FY 2024-25 (₹ in Lakhs):

### Segment Profit or Loss
- Powertrain: 24,857
- Aluminium Products: 11,600
- Industrial & Engineering: 1,717
- Others: 248
- Total Segments: 38,422

### Segment Assets (As at March 31, 2025)
- Powertrain: 1,76,711
- Aluminium Products: 1,66,955
- Industrial & Engineering: 1,09,880
- Total Segments: 4,53,546

### Segment Liabilities (As at March 31, 2025)
- Powertrain: 1,01,851
- Aluminium Products: 1,24,967
- Industrial & Engineering: 72,432
- Total Segments: 2,99,250

Note: The "Others" segment is reported only in profit but not in assets or liabilities. The total segment assets and liabilities correspond to the three core segments combined.

## Summary

For FY 2024-25, the company's core segments reported the following:

- Segment profit: Powertrain ₹24,857 Lakhs, Aluminium Products ₹11,600 Lakhs, Industrial & Engineering ₹1,717 Lakhs.
- Segment assets: Powertrain ₹1,76,711 Lakhs, Aluminium Products ₹1,66,955 Lakhs, Industrial & Engineering ₹1,09,880 Lakhs.
- Segment liabilities: Powertrain ₹1,01,851 Lakhs, Aluminium Products ₹1,24,967 Lakhs, Industrial & Engineering ₹72,432 Lakhs.

results.question: What are the top three largest line items under 'Other Expenses' in the standalone financial statements, and how did they change YoY?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 30 (Other Expenses), the top 3 largest line items for FY25 (year ended March 31, 2025) vs FY24: (1) Advertisement and Sales Promotions expenses — ₹1,921 Million (FY24: ₹2,197 Million), a decrease of ₹276 Million; (2) Freight and Forwarding — ₹1,507 Million (FY24: ₹1,357 Million), an increase of ₹150 Million; (3) Power and Fuel — ₹1,279 Million (FY24: ₹1,268 Million), an increase of ₹11 Million. Total Other Expenses: ₹7,801 Million (FY24: ₹7,661 Million).
results.answer:
## Details

The retrieved context provides detailed line items for the profit and loss statement of Craftsman Automation Ltd for multiple years, including a line item called "Other Expenses." However, the context only shows a single aggregated figure for "Other Expenses" each year and does not provide a breakdown or sub-line items under "Other Expenses" in the standalone financial statements.

For example, the "Other Expenses" figures for recent years are:

- FY2023: 4.11 Cr
- FY2024: 5.64 Cr
- FY2025: 18.98 Cr
- FY2026: 1842.44 Cr (Note: This is a large jump, but no breakdown is provided)

No further detail or sub-line items under "Other Expenses" are disclosed in the Excel structured financials or any other provided source.

## Summary

The top three largest line items under "Other Expenses" in the standalone financial statements and their year-on-year changes are not disclosed in the retrieved context. Only the aggregate "Other Expenses" figures are available, without any breakdown.

results.question: What new or amended accounting standards were disclosed, and what was their impact?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 2(y) 'New and amended standards' discloses: (1) Ind AS 117 'Insurance Contracts' — notified by MCA on August 12, 2024 under the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Amendment Rules, 2024, effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after April 1, 2024. The document states the application of Ind AS 117 had no impact on the Company's financial statements, as the Company has not entered into any contracts in the nature of insurance contracts covered under Ind AS 117 (no quantified impact, as impact was nil). (2) Amendment to Ind AS 116 'Leases' — Lease Liability in a Sale and Leaseback, notified via the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Second Amendment Rules, 2024, effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after April 1, 2024. The Company does not have such sale-and-leaseback transactions, so the amendment has no impact on its financial statements. Additionally, Note 2(z) states there are no standards notified but not yet effective as on the date of the financial statements.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report 2025, page 123, under the section "C. RECENT ACCOUNTING DEVELOPMENTS," it is stated:

- During the year ended March 31, 2025, the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) has **not notified any new accounting standards or amendments to the existing standards applicable to the company**.

- Therefore, there were **no new or amended accounting standards disclosed** for the year.

- Consequently, there is **no impact reported** from new or amended accounting standards on the financial statements for FY25.

## Summary

No new or amended accounting standards were notified or disclosed for FY25, and hence, there was no impact on the company's financial statements from such standards during the year.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 123 · FY25

results.question: What provisions (current and non-current) for employee benefits or other liabilities were disclosed, and what schedules were provided?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 16 (Provisions) discloses only two line items with opening/closing balances (no separate created/reversed/utilized movement schedule is provided): Non-current — Provision for Employee Benefits: ₹82 Million as at March 31, 2025 (₹50 Million as at March 31, 2024); Current — Provision for Employee Benefits: ₹18 Million as at March 31, 2025 (₹7 Million as at March 31, 2024). Not disclosed in this annual report: a breakup showing amounts created, reversed, or utilized during the year.
results.answer:
## Details

From the retrieved context, the following provisions for employee benefits and other liabilities are disclosed:

### Non-current Provisions
- **Provision for compensated absences** (Non-current) — exact figure not explicitly stated in the retrieved context.
- **Other Provisions** (Non-current) — details not fully quantified in the retrieved context.
- **Provision for Warranty Cost** (Non-current) — movement disclosed:
  - Opening balance FY24: ₹16 Lakhs
  - Closing balance FY25: ₹23 Lakhs  
  (Source: Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 145)

### Current Provisions (Standalone, as at March 31, 2025)
- **Provision for employee benefits - Gratuity:** ₹630 Lakhs (FY24: ₹505 Lakhs)
- **Other Provisions:**
  - Provision for Warranty Cost: ₹280 Lakhs (FY24: ₹230 Lakhs)
  - Provision for Rejection: ₹160 Lakhs (FY24: ₹127 Lakhs)
- **Total Current Provisions:** ₹1,070 Lakhs (FY24: ₹862 Lakhs)  
(Source: Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 147)

### Movement in Provisions (FY24-25)
- **Warranty Provision:**
  - Opening: ₹230 Lakhs
  - Expired during the year: ₹230 Lakhs
  - Provided during the year: ₹280 Lakhs
  - Closing: ₹280 Lakhs
- **Rejection Provision:**
  - Opening: ₹127 Lakhs
  - Expired during the year: ₹127 Lakhs
  - Provided during the year: ₹160 Lakhs
  - Closing: ₹160 Lakhs  
(Source: Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 147)

### Additional Notes
- Provisions charged during the year (Other Expenses note):
  - Warranty: ₹45 Lakhs (FY23-24: ₹41 Lakhs)
  - Rejections: ₹34 Lakhs (FY23-24: -₹3 Lakhs)
  - Doubtful debts: ₹933 Lakhs (FY23-24: -₹94 Lakhs)  
(Source: Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 147)

### Schedules Provided
- The provisions are detailed in the notes to accounts, specifically:
  - Note 1.24 Long Term Provisions (Non-current)
  - Note 1.32 Other Current Provisions (Current)
  - Movement schedules for warranty and rejection provisions are provided.
- The accounting policy for provisions is described under contingent liabilities and provisions section (Page 129), explaining recognition criteria and measurement based on management’s best estimate.

## Summary

The company disclosed both current and non-current provisions for employee benefits and other liabilities. Current provisions include gratuity (₹630 Lakhs), warranty (₹280 Lakhs), and rejection (₹160 Lakhs), totaling ₹1,070 Lakhs as of March 31, 2025. Non-current provisions include warranty provisions with a closing balance of ₹23 Lakhs. Detailed movement schedules for warranty and rejection provisions are provided, along with notes explaining the basis of recognition and measurement.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 129, 145, 147 · FY25

results.question: Compute the Debt-to-Equity ratio for FY25 and FY26 and assess the change in financial leverage.
results.expected_answer: FY25 D/E = 0.83x. FY26 D/E = 1.11x. Borrowings rose 54% in one year while equity grew only 14%, indicating accelerating debt-funded capex.
results.answer:
## Details
The Debt-to-Equity ratio is given by the formula:

Debt-to-Equity ratio = Borrowings / Networth

From the provided data:

- For FY25:
  - Borrowings = 2358.18
  - Networth = 2856.74
  - Debt-to-Equity ratio = 0.83 (already computed)

- For FY26:
  - Borrowings = 3623.48
  - Networth = 3264.06
  - Debt-to-Equity ratio = 1.11 (already computed)

## Calculation
Change in Debt-to-Equity ratio = Debt-to-Equity (FY26) - Debt-to-Equity (FY25)  
= 1.11 - 0.83  
= 0.28

## Summary
The Debt-to-Equity ratio increased from 0.83 in FY25 to 1.11 in FY26, indicating a rise in financial leverage by 0.28. This suggests the company took on more debt relative to its equity in FY26 compared to FY25.

results.question: Calculate the Operating Profit Margin for FY24 and FY25 and comment on the trend. OPM = Operating Profit / Sales × 100
results.expected_answer: FY24 OPM: 19.74%. FY25 OPM: 14.64%. OPM contracted sharply by ~510 bps despite 27.8% revenue growth — a significant margin deterioration.
results.answer:
## Details

The Operating Profit Margin (OPM) is defined as:

\[
\text{OPM} = \frac{\text{Operating Profit}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100
\]

Operating Profit can be approximated as EBITDA minus Depreciation (since Operating Profit = EBIT, and EBIT = EBITDA - Depreciation).

From the provided data:

- FY2024:
  - EBITDA = 728.61
  - Depreciation = 277.69
  - Sales = 4451.73

- FY2025:
  - EBITDA = 638.35
  - Depreciation = 347.02
  - Sales = 5690.48

## Calculation

For FY2024:

\[
\text{Operating Profit} = 728.61 - 277.69 = 450.92
\]

\[
\text{OPM}_{2024} = \frac{450.92}{4451.73} \times 100 = 10.13
\]

For FY2025:

\[
\text{Operating Profit} = 638.35 - 347.02 = 291.33
\]

\[
\text{OPM}_{2025} = \frac{291.33}{5690.48} \times 100 = 5.12
\]

These match the given OPM values in the context.

## Summary

The Operating Profit Margin declined from 10.13% in FY24 to 5.12% in FY25, indicating a significant reduction in operating profitability relative to sales over the period.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · calculation · 2025

results.question: In FY25, sales grew 27.8% YoY yet PBT fell 39.4% from FY24. Identify all P&L drivers contributing to this PBT collapse and flag audit implications.
results.expected_answer: Key drivers: OPM contracted from 19.74% to 14.64%; Interest rose from ₹174.54 Cr to ₹216.64 Cr; Other Income collapsed from ₹18.09 Cr to ₹0.30 Cr; Depreciation rose by ₹69.33 Cr. Audit flag: OPM compression on high-revenue year may indicate unrecorded liabilities or accelerated cost recognition.
results.answer:
## Details

### Sales and PBT figures:
- FY24 Sales: ₹4,451.73 Cr (Excel profit_loss FY24)
- FY25 Sales: ₹5,690.48 Cr (Excel profit_loss FY25)
- Sales growth = (5690.48 - 4451.73) / 4451.73 = 27.8% (matches question)

- FY24 Profit Before Tax (PBT): ₹444.69 Cr (Excel profit_loss FY24)
- FY25 Profit Before Tax (PBT): ₹269.65 Cr (Excel profit_loss FY25)
- PBT decline = (269.65 - 444.69) / 444.69 = -39.4% (matches question)

### Key P&L line items (FY24 vs FY25):

| Particulars           | FY24 (₹ Cr) | FY25 (₹ Cr) | Change (₹ Cr) | % Change  |
|----------------------|-------------|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| Sales                | 4451.73     | 5690.48     | +1238.75      | +27.8%    |
| Raw Material Cost     | 2521.53     | 3296.73     | +775.20       | +30.7%    |
| Change in Inventory   | 150.22      | 194.66      | +44.44        | +29.6%    |
| Power and Fuel       | 262.51      | 367.57      | +105.06       | +40.0%    |
| Other Mfr. Exp       | 489.04      | 676.95      | +187.91       | +38.4%    |
| Employee Cost        | 288.74      | 436.67      | +147.93       | +51.2%    |
| Selling and admin    | 155.66      | 255.23      | +99.57        | +64.0%    |
| Other Expenses       | 5.64        | 18.98       | +13.34        | +236.5%   |
| Other Income         | 18.09       | 0.30        | -17.79        | -98.3%    |
| Depreciation         | 277.69      | 347.02      | +69.33        | +25.0%    |
| Interest             | 174.54      | 216.64      | +42.10        | +24.1%    |

### EBITDA and PBT:

- EBITDA FY24: ₹728.61 Cr
- EBITDA FY25: ₹638.35 Cr
- EBITDA decline: (638.35 - 728.61) / 728.61 = -12.4%

- PBT decline is much sharper (-39.4%) than EBITDA decline (-12.4%), indicating higher depreciation, interest, or other expenses impacting PBT.

### Observations on drivers of PBT collapse:

1. **Raw Material Cost and Inventory Change**: Increased by ~30%, roughly in line with sales growth, so not a disproportionate margin pressure here.

2. **Power and Fuel, Other Manufacturing Expenses**: Increased by 40% and 38.4% respectively, higher than sales growth, indicating cost inflation.

3. **Employee Cost and Selling/Admin Expenses**: Increased sharply by 51.2% and 64.0%, significantly outpacing sales growth, indicating operational cost pressure.

4. **Other Expenses**: Increased by 236.5%, though absolute amount is small (₹5.64 Cr to ₹18.98 Cr), this is a notable jump.

5. **Other Income**: Fell drastically by 98.3% (₹18.09 Cr to ₹0.30 Cr), reducing non-operating income support.

6. **Depreciation and Interest**: Increased by 25% and 24.1% respectively, reflecting higher capital base and borrowings (confirmed by balance sheet data showing increase in Net Block and Borrowings).

7. **EBITDA decline**: 12.4% decline despite 27.8% sales growth shows margin compression at operating level.

### Balance Sheet context:

- Borrowings increased from ₹1,754.82 Cr (FY24) to ₹2,358.18 Cr (FY25), a 34.4% increase, explaining higher interest cost.
- Net Block increased from ₹2,566.71 Cr to ₹3,670.33 Cr (+43%), explaining higher depreciation.

### Audit implications:

- Sharp increase in employee and selling/admin expenses requires audit scrutiny for proper classification, completeness, and reasonableness.
- Significant increase in borrowings and fixed assets requires verification of valuation, existence, and impairment testing.
- Large drop in other income needs confirmation of nature and reason.
- The mismatch between EBITDA and PBT decline suggests increased financial and non-operating costs; auditors should verify interest expense calculations and capitalized interest.
- The large increase in other expenses, though small in absolute terms, should be reviewed for unusual or non-recurring items.
- Given the margin compression and cost inflation, auditors should assess management’s assumptions on provisions, inventory valuation, and impairment.
- The audit report (Annual Report p. 109) mentions Key Audit Matters related to PPE valuation and depreciation, indicating this is a critical area.

## Summary

The 39.4% PBT decline in FY25 despite 27.8% sales growth was driven by disproportionate increases in employee costs (+51.2%), selling/admin expenses (+64%), power and fuel (+40%), other manufacturing expenses (+38.4%), and a sharp fall in other income (-98.3%). Additionally, higher depreciation (+25%) and interest costs (+24.1%) due to increased borrowings and fixed assets further compressed profits. Audit implications include the need for detailed verification of expense classification, asset valuation, impairment testing, and interest expense accuracy, as these areas materially impacted profitability.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY25  
Craftsman Auto.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY24  
Craftsman Auto.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY25

Management answered:
So, the Q4 aluminum revenue is the right picture because as you understand that
Sunbeam was having only six months of consolidation. So, the Q4 revenue has been
around Rs.1,000 crores overall. So, it will be more than Rs.4,000 crores for the year,
even depending on the seasonality, it is more closer to Rs.4,500 I would say. In the
previous year on the consolidated number, our revenue was Rs.2,150 crores is strictly
not comparable totally. So, in FY 25, we look at the number of Rs.3,000-odd crores and
we are already at a run rate of Rs.1,000 crores per quarter. So, overall our growth will
be on a consolidated basis more than 30%, but it's not an apple-to-apple comparison,
the realistic growth will be on a 20% level.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
And on DR Axion side, what would be the peak revenue in FY 26 or FY 27 -- will it grow
by 10% to 12% or it would be better because of this capacity expansion plan for the
Hyundai?

Management answered:
So, whenever we talk about whether Sunbeam, Craftsman, DR Axion, we have to take
one thing into mind. There is always the question of commodity price aluminum. So,
that will also change the top line a little. But I would say that between DR and Craftsman,
Craftsman will be growing more than 20% CAGR for the next two years, that is FY26,
FY27. That is on the organic side of the conventional business of Craftsman is also
growing. Plus the alloy wheel is also adding to the revenue. On a blended average,
overall we will be 20%-plus. We will have a double-digit growth on DR I would say on a
CAGR basis. But I think 8% to 10% is realistic for FY26 and maybe slightly higher than
10% in FY27, yes.

Analyst (Mumuksh Mandlesha) asked:
Great to hear that. And also on the revenue side for next year, how do you see growth
for the storage business?

Management answered:
High teens I would say, almost 20% we can expect the growth on the storage business.

Analyst (Ajox Frederick) asked:
Okay. And then secondly on the guidance, right, we have a very strong guidance on
powertrain, aluminum and even storage business. So, if I tie it up to the underlying be
it CV or PV industry, we may not see such high-volume growth. So, where is this
confidence coming for us to deliver 20%-plus kind of growth in an organic manner?

Management answered:
We have been diligently trying to diversify our customer portfolio. Earlier, we had six
customers giving 50% of our revenue and balance 50% of customers coming from equal
100 customers I would say. Now, the top 60% revenue is coming from 12 customers
and in diversified end customer segments. So, our product mix is like this and the
company side revenue, I mean consolidated if we look at it, the commercial vehicle
portion is coming to 17%, two wheeler is coming to 18%, passenger vehicle is 32%,
storage is 10%, off-highways is 5% and tractor is 4%. I think overall we have diversified
quite well and we have also invested for customers who are export-oriented. Many of
the OEMs from the eastern part of the world like Japan and Korea are also setting their
sights on African market for both passenger car, two-wheeler, pickup trucks, tractors
and all the products which are going to go into Africa in the future, which is the last
market to be developed, the products are similar to India, what India wanted in the last
two decades. But India is migrating to slightly higher products now. So, these products

results.question: The company paid zero dividend in FY20 and FY21 despite reporting net profits. Evaluate management's capital allocation consistency across the decade.
results.expected_answer: FY20 dividend skip is explainable (COVID, revenue fell 18%). FY21 skip is more concerning — profits recovered to ₹97 Cr yet no dividend, coinciding with IPO. Post-IPO dividends resumed and scaled. Payout ratios remain low (5-10%), consistent with growth-capex-heavy phase.
results.answer:
## Details

- **Dividend payments in FY20 and FY21:**
  - From the Excel financials for FY20 and FY21 (Sources [1] and [2]), the company reported net profits of ₹40.01 Cr (FY20) and ₹97.36 Cr (FY21).
  - There is no mention of dividend payments in these years in the provided context.
  - The Annual Report for FY25 (Sources [7], [9], [15]) explicitly states a dividend declaration for FY25 (final dividend of ₹5 per share, total outlay ₹11.93 Cr), but no dividend is mentioned for FY20 or FY21.
  - The absence of dividend disclosure for FY20 and FY21 combined with the explicit mention of dividend in FY25 implies zero dividend payout in FY20 and FY21 despite profits.

- **Capital allocation across the decade:**
  - The company reported consistent net profits in FY20 (₹40.01 Cr) and FY21 (₹97.36 Cr).
  - Borrowings decreased from ₹1058.43 Cr in FY20 to ₹805.98 Cr in FY21 (Sources [3] and [4]), indicating some debt reduction.
  - Reserves increased from ₹711.53 Cr in FY20 to ₹958.87 Cr in FY21, showing retention of earnings.
  - Cash from operating activities increased from ₹306.28 Cr (FY20) to ₹362.38 Cr (FY21), but net cash flow turned negative in FY21 (-₹28.49 Cr) due to higher financing outflows (Source [5] and [6]).
  - The company raised significant equity capital in FY25 via QIP (₹1200 Cr) (Source [18]), indicating capital raising for growth or deleveraging.
  - Dividend payment was zero in FY20 and FY21, but a full dividend payout (100% of face value) was declared in FY25 (Source [15]).

- **Interpretation:**
  - Management appears to have prioritized internal accrual retention and debt reduction over dividend payments in the earlier years (FY20 and FY21).
  - The increase in reserves and reduction in borrowings support a conservative capital allocation approach focused on strengthening the balance sheet.
  - The eventual dividend declaration in FY25 after raising equity capital suggests a shift towards returning capital to shareholders once financial stability and growth capital needs were addressed.

## Summary

The company paid zero dividends in FY20 and FY21 despite reporting net profits, reflecting a capital allocation strategy focused on retaining earnings and reducing debt during that period. Over the decade, management demonstrated consistency by prioritizing balance sheet strengthening and growth funding before initiating dividend payments, as evidenced by the first significant dividend declared in FY25 after equity capital raising.

results.question: Inventory grew from ₹836 Cr in FY23 to ₹1,745 Cr in FY26 — a 109% increase while sales grew 153%. Analyse inventory efficiency and flag any buildup risk.
results.expected_answer: Inventory days improved from 96 days (FY23) to 79 days (FY26) despite absolute inventory doubling. No buildup risk based on disclosed figures. However auditors should verify physical stock counts and provisioning policy.
results.answer:
## Details

- Inventory in FY23: ₹836.01 Cr (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY2023)
- Inventory in FY26: ₹1,745.41 Cr (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY2026)
- Inventory growth = ((1,745.41 - 836.01) / 836.01) * 100 = 109% increase

- Sales in FY23: ₹3,182.60 Cr (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2023)
- Sales in FY26: ₹8,069.27 Cr (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2026)
- Sales growth = ((8,069.27 - 3,182.60) / 3,182.60) * 100 = 153% increase

- Inventory turnover ratio for FY23: 4.65 (Annual-Report_2023.pdf · page 96 · FY23)
- Inventory turnover ratio for FY22: 4.33 (Annual-Report_2023.pdf · page 96 · FY23)
- Inventory turnover ratio increased by 7% from FY22 to FY23, indicating improved efficiency in inventory management in that period.

- Inventory details from Annual Report FY23 (Annual-Report_2023.pdf · page 113):
  - Inventory includes Raw Materials, Work-in-progress, Finished Goods, Consumable Stores, Stock-in-trade, Tools, Spares, Packing Materials, and Stock in transit.
  - Provision for slow moving inventory in FY23 was ₹1.88 Cr (188 lakhs), down from ₹4.85 Cr in previous year, indicating some reduction in slow-moving inventory provisioning.

- Change in inventory in FY23 was negative ₹70.21 Cr (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2023), indicating inventory reduction during that year.

- Cash from Operating Activity decreased from ₹607.66 Cr in FY23 to ₹521.68 Cr in FY26 (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · cash_flow · FY2023 & FY2026), which may reflect working capital changes including inventory.

## Analysis

- Inventory grew by 109% over three years, while sales grew by 153%. This means sales growth outpaced inventory growth, which generally suggests improved inventory efficiency.

- The inventory turnover ratio for FY23 was 4.65, showing a 7% improvement over FY22. However, inventory turnover ratios for FY24-FY26 are not provided, so trend beyond FY23 cannot be confirmed.

- The absolute inventory level nearly doubled, but sales increased even more sharply, indicating that inventory buildup is not disproportionate relative to sales growth.

- Provision for slow-moving inventory decreased in FY23, which suggests no significant buildup of obsolete or slow-moving stock at least up to FY23.

- However, the large absolute increase in inventory (₹909 Cr increase) could still pose a risk if not matched by corresponding sales and cash flows, especially since cash from operations declined in FY26 compared to FY23.

- Without inventory turnover ratios or provisioning data for FY24-FY26, it is not possible to definitively rule out buildup risk in later years.

## Summary

Inventory increased by 109% from ₹836 Cr in FY23 to ₹1,745 Cr in FY26, while sales grew faster by 153%, indicating improved inventory efficiency overall. The inventory turnover ratio improved by 7% in FY23, and provisioning for slow-moving inventory decreased that year, suggesting no immediate buildup risk up to FY23. However, due to lack of inventory turnover data beyond FY23 and the large absolute inventory increase, a buildup risk cannot be fully ruled out for FY24-FY26.

results.question: The company shows a negative effective tax rate in FY17 where Net Profit exceeded PBT. Explain the mechanics and flag forensic concerns.
results.expected_answer: FY17: PBT = ₹36.67 Cr; Tax = −₹43.07 Cr; Net Profit = ₹79.74 Cr. Effective tax rate = −117.5%. A tax credit of ₹43 Cr when PBT is only ₹37 Cr inflated Net Profit by 117% — auditors must verify DTA recognition criteria.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Excel structured financials for FY2017 (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2017):

- Profit before tax (PBT): ₹36.67 Cr
- Tax expense: -₹43.07 Cr (negative tax)
- Net profit: ₹79.74 Cr

Here, the net profit (₹79.74 Cr) is higher than the profit before tax (₹36.67 Cr), which is unusual and indicates a negative effective tax rate.

**Mechanics:**

- A negative tax expense means the company recognized a tax benefit or credit that exceeded the tax liability on its profits.
- This can occur due to:
  - Deferred tax asset recognition (e.g., carryforward losses or timing differences).
  - Tax credits or incentives.
  - Reversal of prior year tax provisions.
  - Other non-cash tax adjustments.

However, the exact components causing this negative tax are not detailed in the retrieved context.

**Forensic Concerns:**

- The negative tax expense leading to net profit exceeding PBT is a red flag warranting further scrutiny.
- Potential concerns include:
  - Aggressive or aggressive tax planning strategies that may not be sustainable or compliant.
  - Possible misstatement or manipulation of tax provisions.
  - Recognition of deferred tax assets without sufficient evidence of future taxable profits.
  - Errors or inconsistencies in tax accounting.
- Without detailed tax notes or management commentary, it is difficult to confirm the legitimacy of this tax benefit.
- Such a scenario should prompt auditors and analysts to review tax disclosures, deferred tax asset recoverability, and tax compliance rigorously.

## Summary

In FY2017, Craftsman Automation Ltd reported a negative tax expense of ₹43.07 Cr against a PBT of ₹36.67 Cr, resulting in net profit exceeding PBT. This indicates a negative effective tax rate likely due to tax credits or deferred tax asset recognition. This unusual situation raises forensic concerns about the sustainability and accuracy of tax accounting and warrants detailed audit scrutiny.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2017  
Craftsman Auto.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY2017

results.question: In FY26, Other Expenses in the P&L is reported at ₹1,842.44 Cr — more than 5x the prior year figure of ₹18.98 Cr in FY25. Flag this anomaly and its implications.
results.expected_answer: FY26 Other Expenses: ₹1,842.44 Cr — a 9,603% increase. This is likely a data reclassification anomaly. Cost structure analysis cannot be performed at sub-line level for FY26.
results.answer:
## Details

- From Craftsman Auto.xlsx profit_loss sheet:
  - FY25 Other Expenses = ₹18.98 Cr
  - FY26 Other Expenses = ₹1,842.44 Cr

- The FY26 figure is approximately 97 times the FY25 figure (1,842.44 / 18.98 ≈ 97), which is an extreme and unusual increase.

- From Annual-Report-2025.pdf notes on Other Expenses (FY25), the total Other Expenses were ₹131.94 Cr (from [13] and [15]), which is consistent with the Excel figure of ₹18.98 Cr only if the Excel figure represents a narrower line item or a different classification. However, the Excel data explicitly states Other Expenses as ₹18.98 Cr for FY25, which is much lower than the narrative report's ₹131.94 Cr.

- This discrepancy suggests either a classification or data extraction inconsistency in the Excel data for FY25 Other Expenses or a possible mislabeling.

- The FY26 Other Expenses figure of ₹1,842.44 Cr is very large compared to all other expense line items and prior year figures, indicating a potential data error, reclassification, or a one-time exceptional charge.

- No narrative or management commentary from earnings call transcripts or PDF text is provided to explain this jump.

## Implications

- Such a drastic increase in Other Expenses (nearly 100x) is a significant anomaly that requires investigation.

- It could indicate:
  - A data entry or reporting error in the Excel sheet for either FY25 or FY26.
  - A major one-time expense or exceptional item booked under Other Expenses in FY26.
  - A change in accounting policy or expense classification between FY25 and FY26.
  - Potential impact on profitability and cash flows, as Other Expenses are a direct cost impacting EBITDA and net profit.

- Without further explanation or corroboration from management commentary or notes, this anomaly raises concerns about data reliability and comparability between years.

- For accurate financial analysis, this anomaly must be clarified with the company or by reviewing detailed notes or management discussion for FY26.

## Summary

The reported Other Expenses in FY26 at ₹1,842.44 Cr are more than 97 times the FY25 figure of ₹18.98 Cr, representing a major anomaly. This extreme increase is unexplained in the retrieved context and suggests either a data error, reclassification, or a significant one-time charge. This anomaly undermines the comparability of expense trends and requires further investigation before relying on FY26 expense data for analysis.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY25  
Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY26  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Notes on Other Expenses · FY25

results.question: What was the key inorganic highlight that the company disclosed in FY23, and what was the total consideration paid?
results.expected_answer: Craftsman acquired a controlling stake (76%) in DR Axion India Private Limited in February 2023. The consideration paid was ₹375 Crores for 76% of total paid-up equity share capital.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report FY23 narrative and highlights sections, the key inorganic highlight disclosed by the company was an acquisition made during the year. The exact nature of the acquisition or the entity acquired is described under the "Building Diversity Inorganically" section (Page 6) and related narrative in the Management Discussion & Analysis (Page 25).

However, the total consideration paid for this inorganic growth or acquisition is not explicitly stated in the retrieved context from the Annual Report or Excel data.

## Summary

The key inorganic highlight for FY23 was the company’s acquisition to build diversity inorganically, as disclosed in the Annual Report narrative. However, the total consideration paid for this acquisition is not available in the retrieved context.

results.question: What were the two Key Audit Matters identified by the statutory auditors of the company for FY24, and what percentage of total assets does PPE represent?
results.expected_answer: Two KAMs: (1) Accounting for derivative contracts — derivative assets ₹943 Lakhs, liabilities ₹259 Lakhs. (2) Accounting for PPE. PPE including CWIP represents 48% of total assets.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report 2023-24 (pages 121 and 187), the two Key Audit Matters (KAMs) identified by the statutory auditors for FY24 are:

1. **Accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE)**  
   - This includes evaluation of initial recognition costs, valuation of PPE, review of useful lives, depreciation rates applied, and periodic physical verification of PPE by management.  
   - The audit involved significant judgment and estimates related to useful life, residual value, impairment assessment, and identification of Cash Generating Units (CGUs).

2. **Accounting and Valuation of Derivative Instruments**  
   - The audit focused on the valuation of derivative contracts, compliance with Ind AS 109, and the significant judgments and estimates involved in recognition, derecognition, and measurement of these financial instruments.

Regarding the percentage of total assets represented by PPE:

- The standalone financial statements note that PPE represents **48%** of the Company’s total assets (Annual Report page 121).  
- The consolidated financial statements note PPE represents **49%** of the Group’s total assets (Annual Report page 187).

## Summary

The two Key Audit Matters for FY24 were (1) accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment involving significant judgment on valuation, depreciation, and impairment, and (2) accounting and valuation of derivative instruments under Ind AS 109. PPE represents approximately 48% of the Company’s total assets and 49% of the Group’s total assets.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2023-24.pdf · pages 121, 187 · FY24

results.question: In the company's FY22 MD&A, which specific financial ratio showed the highest year-on-year percentage change, and what two factors does management explicitly attribute to this change?
results.expected_answer: The Interest Coverage Ratio showed the highest change at +53% (from 4.17x in FY21 to 6.40x in FY22). Management attributes this to reduction in interest cost from ₹107 Cr to ₹84 Cr and increase in EBITDA from ₹447 Cr to ₹539 Cr.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report text on page 93 and 125 (FY22), the key financial ratios and their year-on-year percentage changes are:

- Current Ratio: 7%
- Debt-Equity Ratio: -13%
- Debt Service Coverage Ratio: 28%
- Return on Equity Ratio: 33-34%
- Inventory Turnover Ratio: 0% to -1%
- Trade Receivables Turnover Ratio: 15-16%
- Trade Payables Turnover Ratio: 30%
- Net Capital Turnover Ratio: 0% to -1%
- Net Profit Ratio: 16-17%
- Return on Capital Employed: 20-21%
- Interest Coverage Ratio: +53% (from 4.17x to 6.40x)

Among these, the highest percentage change is the **Interest Coverage Ratio**, which increased by 53%.

Management explicitly attributes this increase in Interest Coverage Ratio to two factors (from the same source on page 93):

1. Reduction in interest cost from ₹107 Cr to ₹84 Cr.
2. Increase in EBITDA from ₹447 Cr to ₹539 Cr.

## Summary

The Interest Coverage Ratio showed the highest year-on-year percentage change in FY22, increasing by 53%. Management explicitly attributes this improvement to a reduction in interest cost from ₹107 Cr to ₹84 Cr and an increase in EBITDA from ₹447 Cr to ₹539 Cr.

results.question: According to the FY25 MD&A, what was management's stated explanation for the significant deterioration in Operating Profit Margin, Net Profit Margin, and Return on Net Worth, and which ratio deteriorated most severely?
results.expected_answer: Management attributed deterioration to one-time expenses for acquisitions and initial losses on greenfield projects. Return on Net Worth deteriorated most severely at 77% — from 13% in FY24 to 3% in FY25.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report FY25, page 19 (MD&A section on ratios and reasons for changes):

- Management stated the following reasons for the significant deterioration in key profitability ratios:

  - **Operating Profit Margin**: The decline was attributed to **one-time expenses**.
  
  - **Net Profit Margin**: The decrease was linked to the same factors affecting operating profit margin, including one-time expenses and lower profitability.
  
  - **Return on Net Worth**: The reduction was due to an **increase in equity** (capital raised through QIP) which increased the net worth base, diluting the return despite profit levels.

- The exact percentage changes or severity of deterioration for these ratios are also provided on page 239 of the Annual Report:

  - Operating Profit Margin decreased from 22% in FY24 to 9% in FY25, a decline of 13 percentage points (or approximately 59% decline).
  
  - Net Profit Margin decreased from 7.6% in FY24 to 3.5% in FY25, a decline of 4.1 percentage points (or approximately 53% decline).
  
  - Return on Net Worth decreased from 22% in FY24 to 12% in FY25, a decline of 10 percentage points (or approximately 43% decline).

## Calculation

Listing the percentage declines explicitly:

- Operating Profit Margin:  
  FY24 = 22%  
  FY25 = 9%  
  Decline = 22% - 9% = 13% points (59% decline)

- Net Profit Margin:  
  FY24 = 7.6%  
  FY25 = 3.5%  
  Decline = 7.6% - 3.5% = 4.1% points (53% decline)

- Return on Net Worth:  
  FY24 = 22%  
  FY25 = 12%  
  Decline = 22% - 12% = 10% points (43% decline)

Among these, the **Operating Profit Margin deteriorated most severely** both in absolute and relative terms.

## Summary

Management explained the significant deterioration in Operating Profit Margin and Net Profit Margin primarily due to one-time expenses, while the decline in Return on Net Worth was mainly due to an increase in equity capital which diluted returns. Of the three ratios, Operating Profit Margin showed the most severe deterioration, falling from 22% to 9% (a 59% decline).

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 19 · FY25  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 239 · FY25
