results.question: Across all concall transcripts, management made multiple statements about CAPEX plans. Using Excel investing cash flows and balance sheet changes, quantify how much of the guided CAPEX was actually deployed each year.
results.expected_answer: Concall guided FY23 CAPEX ₹225-275 Cr. Excel investing outflows show actual capex ~₹856 Cr — significantly exceeding guidance. Management underguided by ~3x, partly explained by the DR Axion acquisition (₹375 Cr) included in investing outflows.
results.answer:
## Details

- Management's CAPEX guidance from the FY25 earnings call transcript (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 7 · FY25) is around Rs. 750 to 800 crores for the group.

- To quantify actual CAPEX deployed each year, we need to use Excel investing cash flows or balance sheet changes related to fixed assets or capital expenditure. However, the retrieved context does not include any Excel sheet or data specifically showing investing cash flows or fixed asset additions/disposals.

- The Excel profit & loss sheets (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss) provide no direct investing cash flow or CAPEX figures.

- The Annual Report notes and balance sheet excerpts provided (Annual-Report-2025.pdf) mention lease liabilities and right-of-use assets but do not disclose explicit CAPEX amounts or fixed asset additions.

- No explicit investing cash flow or CAPEX figures are present in the retrieved context for any year.

- Therefore, it is not possible to quantify the actual CAPEX deployed each year from the retrieved context.

## Summary

The retrieved context does not contain explicit investing cash flow data or fixed asset additions to quantify the actual CAPEX deployed each year. Hence, it is not possible to compare the management's CAPEX guidance with actual CAPEX deployed using the available data.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 7 · FY25  
Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY17-FY26  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 122, 156, 209, 212, 224, 235 · FY25

results.question: The FY25 MD&A attributes RoNW collapse of 77% to one-time expenses for acquisitions. Using Excel, calculate RoNW for FY24 and FY25 independently and assess whether the one-time explanation is sufficient.
results.expected_answer: FY25 PAT = ₹194.57 Cr, Networth = ₹2,856.74 Cr. RoNW FY25 = 6.8% vs FY24 ~13%. Management's one-time framing is partially misleading — the QIP equity dilution mechanically depressed RoNW regardless of one-time charges.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Excel financial data:

- Net Profit:
  - FY24: ₹304.47 Cr (Source [1])
  - FY25: ₹194.57 Cr (Source [2])

- Net Worth:
  - FY24: ₹1657.98 Cr (Source [3])
  - FY25: ₹2856.74 Cr (Source [4])

RoNW (Return on Net Worth) = (Net Profit / Net Worth) × 100

Calculate RoNW for both years:

- FY24 RoNW = (304.47 / 1657.98) × 100 = 18.36%
- FY25 RoNW = (194.57 / 2856.74) × 100 = 6.81%

Change in RoNW = ((6.81 - 18.36) / 18.36) × 100 = -62.9% decline

From the Annual Report text (Source [7]):

- The MD&A attributes the collapse in RoNW by 77% to one-time expenses related to acquisitions (M&A work including takeovers of DR Axion, Sunbeam, Fronberg).

From the Excel data, the RoNW decline is about 62.9%, not 77%.

Also, note:

- Net Worth increased significantly from ₹1657.98 Cr to ₹2856.74 Cr (an increase of about 72%), which dilutes RoNW if net profit does not increase proportionally.
- Net Profit declined from ₹304.47 Cr to ₹194.57 Cr (a decline of about 36%).

One-time expenses related to acquisitions are confirmed in the earnings call and annual report (Source [12]), which increased expenses in FY25.

## Summary

The RoNW declined from 18.36% in FY24 to 6.81% in FY25, a drop of approximately 63%, not 77% as stated in the MD&A. The significant increase in net worth and the decline in net profit both contributed to this drop. The one-time acquisition-related expenses partially explain the net profit decline, but the large increase in net worth also diluted RoNW. Therefore, the one-time expense explanation alone is insufficient to fully account for the RoNW collapse.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Okay, sir. And my last question is on the loan borrowing side. Now we have around
Rs.1,900 Crores kind of debt in books. So, what is your deleveraging plan for the next
two to three years?

Management answered:
So, if you look at it, the cash run rate generation will be quite high even in the next
financial year plus aided by possibly the land sale in Q3 or Q4 of Sunbeam, that will
add around Rs.300 crores plus depending on that market value at that particular point
of time. Our CAPEX guidance as a whole for a group will be only around Rs.750 crores
to Rs.800 crores totally in that region.

Analyst (Mumuksh Mandlesha) asked:
Got it, sir. Sir, this quarter, the storage, I mean the industrial segment margins have
seen a lot of improvement in Q4. Can you explain what led to the improvement there?
And going ahead for the next year how do you see the margin for this business, sir?

Management answered:
See, you may be aware of this, but I would like to reiterate one particular point on the
storage business. This is not at all CAPEX-intensive, there is CAPEX, but compared to
the automotive business on both aluminum and powertrain, this is a negligible CAPEX
I would say and working capital also the total capital employed is quite low. So, even
though margins may be low, once we see the traction what we got in Q4, which will
continue into the next financial year, of course, there can be some blips in quarter-to-
quarter but overall we can expect the results for FY26 will be better than the results of
Q4 of FY25. And the reason for that is the automated storage division is getting new
orders because we have made enough inroads into the market, we have enough
projects which have been implemented to showcase to our new incoming customers
and we are getting the right pricing for the product, #1. #2, I think we also have
rationalized the product costing and also on the static racking we are having good
traction there. So, as a blended Company, I think we have matured as a storage solution
Company. This is a very difficult business. Within five years we have come to a level.
This can be really a standalone also possible storage solution business. Not that I'm
implying anything, no, please don't take it that level, but we have grown this business
to be able to stand on its own feet now.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Rs.750 crores in FY26?

Management answered:
Yes, on a group level and group level EBITDA of upward of Rs.1,100 crores and a land
sale of Rs.300 crores, depreciation is around Rs.450 crores, there will be some interest
outflow. And for Sunbeam there will be no tax, but for DR and Craftsman, there will be
taxes.

results.question: Management guided debt reduction as a priority across multiple concall years. The FY25 MD&A shows Debt-Equity Ratio improving to 0.59x due to QIP. Using Excel borrowings data across all years, did debt actually reduce in absolute terms?
results.expected_answer: Excel Borrowings: FY22 ₹799 Cr → FY23 ₹1,240 Cr → FY24 ₹1,754 Cr → FY25 ₹2,358 Cr → FY26 ₹3,623 Cr. Debt rose every single year in absolute terms. The FY25 D/E improvement is entirely driven by QIP equity raise, not debt reduction. Management's narrative is factually contradicted by Excel data.
results.answer:
## Details

- From Craftsman Auto.xlsx balance_sheet for FY2025:
  - Borrowings = ₹2358.18 Cr
  - Networth = ₹2856.74 Cr
  - Debt-Equity Ratio = Borrowings / Networth = 2358.18 / 2856.74 ≈ 0.825x (Excel data)

- From Annual Report PDF notes (FY25) and related sheets:
  - The MD&A states Debt-Equity Ratio improved to 0.59x due to QIP (Qualified Institutional Placement).
  - However, the Excel data shows Borrowings at ₹2358.18 Cr and Networth at ₹2856.74 Cr, which gives a ratio of ~0.825x, not 0.59x.
  - This suggests the MD&A ratio might be standalone or adjusted differently, but Excel data is authoritative for absolute borrowings.

- To check if debt reduced in absolute terms, we need borrowings from prior years from Excel data:

  Unfortunately, the retrieved context only provides borrowings for FY2025 (₹2358.18 Cr). Borrowings for previous years are not explicitly provided in the retrieved Excel data or any other source.

- Management in earnings call (crafts25Q4.pdf) mentioned a deleveraging plan and expects high cash generation plus land sale proceeds to reduce debt, but this is forward-looking guidance, not past actuals.

## Summary

The absolute borrowings figure for FY2025 is ₹2358.18 Cr as per Excel data. However, borrowings data for prior years is not available in the retrieved context, so it is not possible to determine from the provided data whether debt actually reduced in absolute terms over the years despite management guidance and MD&A commentary on improving Debt-Equity ratio.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY25  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 168, 234 · FY25  
crafts25Q4.pdf · Pages 5, 12 · FY25

results.question: The FY24 KPI page bar chart shows Debt-EBITDA rising for two consecutive years to 1.72x in FY24. Using Excel, extend this calculation to FY25 and FY26. Does the trend accelerate, stabilise, or reverse?
results.expected_answer: Excel FY25: Borrowings ₹2,358 Cr, EBITDA ₹833 Cr → D/EBITDA = 2.83x. The trend shown in the FY24 chart dramatically accelerates in FY25 — a fact not visible in the annual report image which only shows through FY24.
results.answer:
## Details

The Debt-EBITDA ratio is calculated as:

\[
\text{Debt-EBITDA ratio} = \frac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{EBITDA}}
\]

From the context:

- For FY24 (from Annual-Report-2023-24.pdf, Page 21 and 14), Debt-EBITDA ratio is given as 1.72x.
- Using Excel data for FY24, FY25, and FY26:

### Debt (Borrowings) from Balance Sheet (Excel):

- FY24 Borrowings = ₹1,754.82 Cr
- FY25 Borrowings = ₹2,358.18 Cr
- FY26 Borrowings = ₹3,623.48 Cr

### EBITDA from Profit & Loss (Excel):

- FY24 EBITDA = ₹728.61 Cr
- FY25 EBITDA = ₹638.35 Cr
- FY26 EBITDA = ₹1,114.16 Cr

Note: The EBITDA from Excel differs from the Annual Report image (which shows 645 Cr for FY24), but per Rule 6, Excel data is authoritative for precise figures.

## Calculation

Calculate Debt-EBITDA ratio for FY24, FY25, and FY26:

- FY24 Debt-EBITDA = 1754.82 / 728.61 = 2.41x
- FY25 Debt-EBITDA = 2358.18 / 638.35 = 3.69x
- FY26 Debt-EBITDA = 3623.48 / 1114.16 = 3.25x

## Summary

Using Excel data, the Debt-EBITDA ratio rose sharply from 2.41x in FY24 to 3.69x in FY25, indicating an accelerating increase in leverage relative to EBITDA. In FY26, the ratio declined slightly to 3.25x, showing a partial reversal but still remaining significantly higher than FY24. Thus, the trend accelerates from FY24 to FY25 and then partially reverses in FY26 but remains elevated.

Analyst (Karan Gupta) asked:
Okay. But any guidance on cash flow generation from these two plants, what we are
estimating because currently we are in the run rate of around INR500 crores of cash
flow. What additional incremental cash flow we are estimating from these two?

Management answered:
So if you look at our debt itself, we have minused the INR375 crores invested in the DR
Axion and minus the two plants what we invested roughly -- around INR100 crores
already invested into the two plants put together in the last year financial itself. Then
you remove that INR475 crores and overall, I think organically our debt would have
been very low if not for these sort of investments. And we have borne the interest also
for the DR Axion acquisition that is also depressed our PBT in a way, stand-alone PBT,
but look at the consolidated PBT would have benefited from that.

Analyst (Capital. Please go ahead.
Karan Gupta) asked:
Hi. Again, the question related to the previous question. So we are raising maybe debt
over the next couple of quarters, maybe, which is not sure. But as of now, we have the
debt. So what is the plan of deleverage the balance sheet? That is one. Second is, what
is the cash flow we are estimating from these two new plants, which will cover our debt
over the period of time without raising much external debt? So that is the kind of
blueprint I am asking.

Management answered:
Thank you, Mr. Karan Gupta, on this question. Now we started during an IPO stage of
around INR1,400 crores top line and our consolidated revenue was nothing different
from the top line, totally. So now we are at a different number and there is a big outflow
towards the acquisition of DR Axion INR375 crores money has left the company to
create an asset, which is also giving a good return on our investment, totally.
So the debt portion has been towards a good.. allocation of capital, we are very, very,
very careful on this matter. And today, when we are doing some organic growth, unless
we have that sort of capacities, the global opportunity is going to slip. With a base of
currently on a top line of around INR4,500 crores, how do we grow at a double digit
growth? This is quite substantial going forward, unless we are ready with the investment
there. So I request your kind understanding of that subject.
Now what happens if we don't grow or we grow late? We grow late, we may miss the
bus on the business. As and when we get an opportunity, we have to grab that
opportunity and move ahead. Otherwise, we will be stuck with the growth of the existing
customers, where then we have to answer that our customers did not pick up the
material. So we have to create new customer, new avenues for growth so that we can
sustain this growth.
The other thing is a double-edged sword, no growth means we can be attacked on the
inflationary angle. So on the old products, always there is a risk of declining margins
over a period of time because of the inflationary pressure, totally. So we need to get
new businesses at new prices and emerging businesses also. So that is the reason we
want to keep this and we are very close to -- I don't want to talk about number, I think
we are not far away in the next year to a INR1,000 crores EBITDA number.

Karan Gupta

Yes. FY '25, we'll be looking at a capex. We'll be completing the stage one of both the

See, the aluminum prices, I mean, as I mentioned, the base metal price now it is

inventories are covering all the three segments of the business on the consolidated
base. It is not only that. We have a lot of steel also for our storage solution business
which is long gestation, the variety of steel is large. So, it will not have any impact on
the profitability per se, but maybe the – optically the EBITDA margin may come down,
but I don't see any difference on the return on capital or the profitability.

results.question: The FY23 auditor's report identifies derivative contracts as a KAM with assets of ₹936 Lakhs and liabilities of ₹375 Lakhs. In the FY23 Q2 concall, management discussed aluminum price risk. Are these derivative positions consistent with the hedging strategy described?
results.expected_answer: Auditor's KAM shows net derivative asset of ₹561 Lakhs — a modest position vs ₹196 Cr quarterly aluminum revenue. The derivatives appear to be forex hedges rather than commodity hedges, meaning aluminum price risk was genuinely unhedged as management implied in the concall.
results.answer:
## Details

- From the FY23 auditor's report (Annual-Report_2023.pdf · Page 63), derivative contracts are identified as a Key Audit Matter (KAM) with derivative assets of ₹936 Lakhs and derivative liabilities of ₹375 Lakhs.
- The audit notes specify that the derivatives include currency swaps and interest rate swaps, with currency swaps being cash flow hedges carried at fair value through OCI, and interest rate swaps and collars also carried at fair value through OCI if hedge effective, otherwise through P&L (Annual-Report_2023.pdf · Page 116).
- The derivatives relate primarily to borrowings, which are denominated in Indian rupee and foreign currency, with a combination of fixed and floating interest rates (Annual-Report_2023.pdf · Page 63).
- The maturity profile and classification of derivatives show currency and interest rate swaps and options, with no mention of commodity derivatives (Annual-Report_2023.pdf · Page 125).
- In the FY23 Q2 earnings call transcript (crafts23Q2.pdf · Pages 4, 6, 12, 13, 19), management discusses aluminum price risk and its impact on margins, explaining the lag in passing on aluminum price changes to customers and the resulting margin pressure.
- However, there is no mention in the concall or audit report of derivatives used to hedge aluminum price risk or commodity price risk.
- The derivatives disclosed and audited are related to currency and interest rate risk management, not commodity price risk.

## Summary

The derivative positions disclosed in the FY23 auditor's report (currency swaps and interest rate swaps) are consistent with hedging foreign exchange and interest rate risks related to borrowings. However, these derivatives do not cover aluminum price risk, which management discussed in the FY23 Q2 concall. Therefore, the derivative positions are not consistent with hedging the aluminum commodity price risk described by management.

## Sources

Annual-Report_2023.pdf · Pages 63, 116, 125 · FY23  
crafts23Q2.pdf · Pages 4, 6, 12, 13, 19 · FY23

results.question: Management stated in multiple concalls that the Automotive Powertrain segment is the core earnings driver. Using Excel and the FY24 MD&A segment EBIT data, calculate what percentage of total company EBIT came from Powertrain in FY22 and FY24.
results.expected_answer: FY22: Powertrain EBIT ~₹333 Cr representing nearly 100% of total EBIT as other segments were near breakeven. FY24: Powertrain EBIT contribution declining as Aluminum (DR Axion) grew. The segment's EBIT dominance is clearly declining — management's concall narrative of Powertrain as core is becoming less numerically accurate.
results.answer:
## Details

From the context, we have the following data:

### FY24 Segment EBIT Data (from Annual Report PDF notes to consolidated financials, page 248):

- Segment Profit or Loss (in Lakhs):
  - Powertrain: 29,163
  - Aluminium Products: 32,203
  - Industrial & Engineering: 4,935
- Total Segments EBIT = 29,163 + 32,203 + 4,935 = 66,301 Lakhs
- Less: Unallocable Expenditure (not explicitly stated here, but EBIT is given after this adjustment)
- Add: Other Income (not explicitly stated here)
- Earnings Before Interest & Tax (EBIT) = 66,301 Lakhs (total segments EBIT before unallocable adjustments)
- Finance Costs and Profit Before Tax are given but not needed for EBIT percentage calculation.

Since the exact EBIT after unallocable adjustments is not explicitly stated, we will use the total segments EBIT as the total EBIT for the company for FY24, as the question asks for segment EBIT as a percentage of total company EBIT and the only EBIT figure available is the total segments EBIT.

### FY22 EBIT Data (from Excel profit_loss sheet):

- EBIT is not directly stated, but can be calculated as:
  
  EBIT = Profit before tax + Interest expense  
  From FY22 profit_loss:
  - Profit before tax = 251.73 Cr  
  - Interest = 84.22 Cr  
  So, EBIT FY22 = 251.73 + 84.22 = 335.95 Cr

- Total company EBIT FY22 = 335.95 Cr

### FY22 Powertrain EBIT:

- Not directly given in Excel or PDF for FY22.

- The segment EBIT data for FY22 is not available in the retrieved context.

### FY24 Total Company EBIT:

- From Excel profit_loss FY24:

  - Profit before tax = 444.69 Cr  
  - Interest = 174.54 Cr  
  So, EBIT FY24 = 444.69 + 174.54 = 619.23 Cr

- From PDF segment data, total segments EBIT = 66,301 Lakhs = 663.01 Cr

- There is a discrepancy between Excel EBIT (619.23 Cr) and PDF segment EBIT (663.01 Cr). According to Rule 6 (conflicting data sources), Excel data is authoritative for precise financial figures.

- Therefore, total company EBIT FY24 = 619.23 Cr (Excel data)

- Segment EBIT for Powertrain FY24 = 29,163 Lakhs = 291.63 Cr (from PDF segment data)

- Since segment EBIT is not available in Excel, we use PDF segment EBIT for Powertrain and Excel total EBIT for company.

### Calculation of Powertrain EBIT % of total EBIT:

- FY24:

  Powertrain EBIT = 291.63 Cr  
  Total EBIT = 619.23 Cr

- FY22:

  Powertrain EBIT not available in retrieved context.

## Calculation

FY24 Powertrain EBIT % = (Powertrain EBIT / Total EBIT) × 100  
= (291.63 / 619.23) × 100  
= 47.1%

FY22 Powertrain EBIT % = Not available (Powertrain segment EBIT for FY22 is not disclosed in the retrieved context)

## Summary

In FY24, the Automotive Powertrain segment contributed approximately 47.1% of the total company EBIT. The Powertrain segment EBIT for FY22 is not available in the retrieved context, so the percentage for FY22 cannot be calculated.

Analyst (Mukesh Saraf) asked:
If you could give us both, sir, 4Q and full year.

Management answered:
Okay. The powertrain revenue-wise for commercial vehicle has been around INR880
crores. Off-highway was INR272 crores and the tractor was INR210 crores and the SUV
for the passenger vehicles was INR196 crores. And for Q4 of FY '24 on commercial
vehicle, it was INR217 crores, off-highway was INR63 crores, tractor was INR48 crores
and passenger vehicle SUV was INR61 crores.

Analyst (Basudeb Banerjee) asked:
And second thing is too much of dichotomy on one side, so much news on government
capex, infra projects, metal mining doing well. And on the other side, commercial vehicle
demand remaining subdued almost for preceding six months. And Outlook is also
subdued for at least the foreseeable future. So, being a key supplier to commercial
vehicle cylinder blocks, how do you see demand Outlook from the key CV OEMs in
whatever period you can explain to me?

Management answered:
While I mentioned on FY'25 and most part of FY'26 also will be more or less as you had
mentioned what I had also – I foresee on this matter. But the next change has to come
on the higher horsepower engines. If you look at all the developed markets, they're
operating between 350 to 450 horsepower engines. Currently, all of our engines are –
most of – 90% of engines are below 250 horsepower. So I see that as a step change.
This means bigger engines, bigger gearboxes, more fuel-efficient trucks per kilometer
covered on this matter.
Higher torque on the gearboxes today, 1,300 newton meter is what is the maximum
here in India. And most of them are operating at 1,100 newton meter. So we see that
in the global scale, 1,600 newton meter. And the – any commercial vehicle, whether it
is even countries like of course China, but also in countries like Brazil, also they have
moved to 13-liter engine long back for a major portion of the requirement. They don't
call a 300 horsepower truck as a heavy-duty truck. So this is the big growth driver from
FY'27 onwards for Craftsman when this change starts to take place.
But meantime, yes, there will be subdued growth on the powertrain business for this
segment, I would say, the commercial vehicle segment. But it will start. I hope I
answered that holistically.

Analyst (Please go ahead.
Mukesh Saraf) asked:
Yes. Good evening, and thank you for the opportunity. My first question is on the
aluminum segment. Within the standalone business, we've seen some sequential
growth in revenue. Could you give some sense on, have you started seeing some
contribution from some of those export orders that we had to Europe? Have you started
seeing some of those, and could this ramp up further in these couple of coming
quarters?

Management answered:
I mean, regarding export orders for the PV, it is starting only from Q1. This has just
started now, little. So, we will be seeing offtake from Q2 of this financial year, and it will
peak in Q3, Q4 is what we expect on this matter. That is what is the projections we
received. We have not seen much in the Q4 of last year on the passenger vehicle
segment for export. But domestically, our business for the powertrain, for the passenger
vehicles, for the standalone, we have started production in the Q2, a little.
Q3 was the ramp-up phase, and Q4 was the – we have supplied at full quantity that
resulted in the numbers. That is going to continue for the full year, financial year, and
for the next year. So, in added to that, I think the export revenue will add to the
aluminum. So, aluminum traction is quite strong, similar to the current year. I mean, last
year, the current year also looks strong, both for Craftsman as well as for DR.

results.question: The FY25 Directors' Report discloses loans and advances to related parties jumped from 0% to 100% per BRSR data. Using Excel balance sheet data, assess whether this represents a material fund flow to subsidiaries.
results.expected_answer: The 0% to 100% RPT concentration shift combined with three acquisitions in FY25 (DR Axion, Sunbeam, Craftsman Germany) suggests the intercompany loans are acquisition-related funding. Absolute quantum from Excel determines materiality under IND AS 24.
results.answer:
## Details

- From the Annual Report related party transactions (Source [6]), the year-end balance of loans receivable from subsidiaries as of March 31, 2025, is ₹6,640 Lakhs (₹66.40 Cr).
- The Excel balance sheet data for FY25 (Source [2]) shows:
  - Total Other Assets: ₹3,090.24 Cr
  - Receivables: ₹920.59 Cr
  - Inventory: ₹1,332.05 Cr
  - Cash & Bank: ₹123.23 Cr
  - Net Block: ₹3,670.33 Cr
  - Borrowings: ₹2,358.18 Cr
  - Networth: ₹2,856.74 Cr

- The loans receivable from subsidiaries (₹66.40 Cr) form part of the financial assets or advances but the exact line item "Loans and Advances to Related Parties" is not separately disclosed in the Excel balance sheet. However, the ₹66.40 Cr figure is explicitly stated as loans receivable from subsidiaries in the related party note.

- Comparing ₹66.40 Cr loans receivable to total assets or other relevant balance sheet items:
  - Total assets are not explicitly stated, but summing key assets from balance sheet:
    - Net Block: ₹3,670.33 Cr
    - Capital Work in Progress: ₹345.26 Cr
    - Investments: ₹10.61 Cr
    - Other Assets: ₹3,090.24 Cr
    - Receivables: ₹920.59 Cr
    - Inventory: ₹1,332.05 Cr
    - Cash & Bank: ₹123.23 Cr
  - Total assets (approximate sum) = 3,670.33 + 345.26 + 10.61 + 3,090.24 + 920.59 + 1,332.05 + 123.23 = ₹9,492.31 Cr

- Loans to subsidiaries (₹66.40 Cr) as a percentage of total assets:
  - (66.40 / 9492.31) * 100 = 0.70%

- Loans to subsidiaries as a percentage of net worth (₹2,856.74 Cr):
  - (66.40 / 2856.74) * 100 = 2.32%

- Loans to subsidiaries as a percentage of cash from operating activities (₹283.33 Cr):
  - (66.40 / 283.33) * 100 = 23.44%

- The loans receivable from subsidiaries increased from 0 in the prior year (per the question statement and related party note showing no prior year loans) to ₹66.40 Cr in FY25.

## Summary

The loans and advances to related parties (₹66.40 Cr) represent a new and material fund flow to subsidiaries in FY25, rising from zero previously. While this amount is a small portion of total assets (0.7%) and net worth (2.3%), it is significant relative to operating cash flow (23.4%), indicating a notable deployment of funds to subsidiaries during the year.

results.question: Across all available sources — Excel, concalls, annual report visuals, and MD&A ratios — construct a complete picture of Craftsman's debt trajectory from FY22 to FY26. Did management's stated commitment to deleveraging materialise?
results.expected_answer: Concall guidance: multiple commitments to ₹100 Cr debt reduction. PDF visuals: FY22 KPI chart shows D/EBITDA at 1.33x — lowest in 5 years. MD&A FY25: D/E improved to 0.59x via QIP. Excel raw data: Borrowings FY22 ₹799 Cr → FY26 ₹3,623 Cr — a 353% absolute increase. Every narrative source suggests deleveraging intent but Excel shows continuous accelerating debt accumulation. The ratio improvement in FY25 is entirely a QIP artifact.
results.answer:
## Details

### Debt figures from Excel balance sheets:
- FY22 Borrowings: ₹799.55 Cr (Craftsman Auto.xlsx | balance_sheet | FY2022)
- FY26 Borrowings: ₹3623.48 Cr (Craftsman Auto.xlsx | balance_sheet | FY2026)

### Management commentary on debt from FY22 earnings call:
- As of March 2022, total debt stood at ₹713 Cr (crafts22Q4.pdf | Page 10 | FY22 concall)
- Long-term debt reduced from ₹546 Cr (previous year) to ₹522 Cr (March 2022)
- Working capital loan increased from ₹150 Cr to ₹193 Cr
- Management expected debt to reduce by around ₹100 Cr in FY23 through term loan and working capital loan reduction

### Debt ratios and analysis from FY22 Annual Report:
- Debt-Equity Ratio FY22: 0.63 (7.-Annual-Report-2021-22.pdf | Page 93)
- Debt-Equity Ratio FY21: 0.72 (7.-Annual-Report-2021-22.pdf | Page 93)
- Debt Service Coverage Ratio FY22: 1.70 (improved from 1.32 in FY21)
- Debt/EBITDA FY22: 1.43 (7.-Annual-Report-2021-22.pdf | Page 9)
- Net worth FY22: ₹1135.74 Cr (Craftsman Auto.xlsx | balance_sheet | FY2022)
- Net worth FY26: ₹3264.06 Cr (Craftsman Auto.xlsx | balance_sheet | FY2026)

### Observations on debt trajectory:
- Borrowings increased sharply from ₹799.55 Cr in FY22 to ₹3623.48 Cr in FY26 (Excel data)
- Net worth also increased from ₹1135.74 Cr to ₹3264.06 Cr in the same period
- Debt-Equity ratio in FY22 was 0.63, indicating moderate leverage at that time
- Management expected debt reduction in FY23, but Excel data shows a large increase in borrowings by FY26
- Cash flow from financing activity in FY26 was positive ₹827.46 Cr, indicating new borrowings or capital inflows (Craftsman Auto.xlsx | cash_flow | FY2026)
- Cash flow from financing activity in FY22 was negative ₹120.04 Cr, indicating debt repayment or outflows

## Summary

Despite management's stated commitment in FY22 to reduce debt by around ₹100 Cr in FY23, the overall debt (borrowings) of Craftsman Automation Ltd increased significantly from ₹799.55 Cr in FY22 to ₹3623.48 Cr in FY26 according to Excel balance sheet data. This indicates that the deleveraging commitment did not materialize over the medium term. While net worth also grew substantially, the sharp rise in borrowings suggests increased leverage rather than sustained debt reduction.

results.answer:
## Details

Capital Expenditure (Capex) is typically reflected in the cash flow from investing activities as outflows related to the purchase of fixed assets (net block additions or capital work in progress increases).

From the cash flow data for FY25 (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · cash_flow · 2025):

- Cash from Investing Activity: -1642.82 Cr

This figure represents net cash used in investing activities, which generally includes purchase of fixed assets (Capex), proceeds from sale of fixed assets, investments, and other investing cash flows.

However, the exact amount of Capital Expenditure (purchase of fixed assets) is not separately disclosed in the retrieved context. The cash flow from investing activity is a net figure and may include other investing cash flows besides Capex.

No explicit line item or breakdown for Capital Expenditure is provided in the Excel sheets or other sources.

## Summary

The exact Capital Expenditure (purchase of fixed assets) for Craftsman Automation in FY25 is not explicitly disclosed in the retrieved context. Only the net cash outflow from investing activities of ₹1642.82 Cr is available, which may include Capex but is not solely Capex.

results.question: What is the current ratio for Craftsman Automation for FY25 and FY26?
results.expected_answer: Not computable from this dataset. The Balance Sheet only provides aggregate line items (Borrowings, Other Liabilities, Net Block, CWIP, Investments, Other Assets, Cash & Bank). Current Assets and Current Liabilities are not separately disclosed.
results.answer:
## Details

The current ratio is calculated as:

**Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities**

From the balance sheet data for FY25 and FY26, we need to identify current assets and current liabilities.

- Current Assets typically include:  
  - Receivables  
  - Inventory  
  - Cash & Bank  

- Current Liabilities are not explicitly labeled in the provided data. The balance sheet shows:  
  - Borrowings  
  - Other Liabilities  

Since the exact line item "Current Liabilities" is not disclosed in the retrieved context, and "Other Liabilities" and "Borrowings" are broad categories that may include both current and non-current portions, we cannot use them as a substitute for current liabilities (per Rule 2: NO PROXY SUBSTITUTIONS).

Therefore, the exact current liabilities figure is not available.

Current Assets for FY25 and FY26 can be summed as:

- FY25 Current Assets = Receivables (920.59 Cr) + Inventory (1332.05 Cr) + Cash & Bank (123.23 Cr) = 2375.87 Cr  
- FY26 Current Assets = Receivables (1110.54 Cr) + Inventory (1745.41 Cr) + Cash & Bank (178.78 Cr) = 3034.73 Cr

But since current liabilities are not disclosed, the current ratio cannot be calculated.

## Summary

The current ratio for Craftsman Automation for FY25 and FY26 cannot be calculated because the exact figure for current liabilities is not disclosed in the retrieved context. Although current assets can be computed, the absence of current liabilities data prevents the calculation.

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY2025

results.question: Calculate the Payable Days for Craftsman Automation for FY25 and FY26 and compute the full Cash Conversion Cycle.
results.expected_answer: Payable Days not computable. The Balance Sheet has only an aggregate 'Other Liabilities' line — no distinct Trade Payables figure. Without Payable Days, the Cash Conversion Cycle cannot be computed. Receivable Days and Inventory Days are individually computable but CCC requires all three components.
results.answer:
## Details

The question requires calculation of Payable Days and the full Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) for FY25 and FY26 for Craftsman Automation.

To calculate Payable Days, the formula is:

\[
\text{Payable Days} = \frac{\text{Trade Payables}}{\text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)}} \times 365
\]

To calculate the full Cash Conversion Cycle, the formula is:

\[
\text{CCC} = \text{Inventory Days} + \text{Receivable Days} - \text{Payable Days}
\]

However, the context provided does not include any data on Trade Payables, Cost of Goods Sold, Inventory, or Receivables for FY25 or FY26. Without these specific line items, it is not possible to calculate Payable Days or the full Cash Conversion Cycle.

The only data available relates to Cash from Operating Activity and Net Profit for FY25 and FY26, which is unrelated to the calculation of Payable Days or CCC.

## Summary

The required line items (Trade Payables, COGS, Inventory, Receivables) to calculate Payable Days and the full Cash Conversion Cycle for FY25 and FY26 are not disclosed in the provided context. Therefore, the calculation cannot be performed.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · calculation · 2026

Analyst (Akshay Bhor) asked:
What I mean is that the production levels as compared to Q1 FY20 are significantly down for
the OEMs be it truck manufacturers or CVs or PVs or two-wheelers. What is this out-
performance about? Is it just a BS-VI transition or there is something?

Management answered:
No, it is not a BS-VI transition alone, that is adding to that the product mix has changed. As
Craftsman is in the machining segment, we are looking at more critical parts and there, the value
addition is keeping on improving. We are looking at direct exports, tier-II exports, everything
on the machining. There is not one significant order or something like that. There are many
product lines on a continuous development. I will not be able to say this is 1-2-3, just like I talked
about the auto aluminium but many of the projects have started production, I would say. That is
the reason for the outperformance when compared to the industry in the auto powertrain. If you
look from FY16 onwards, we have been continuously outperforming on the auto powertrain
when compared to the industry.

Analyst (Dhaval Shah) asked:
Now coming on the main business, on the powertrain business what sort of outlook are you
having from the M&HCV and other customer side?

Management answered:
As a whole, in Q1 our operating leverage itself was very low and as the operating leverage is
kicking in better in the month of July and as we move forward it'll improve month on month.
The one thing is the capacity utilization as it increases our fixed cost absorption will better and
this will lead to better margins going forward. But we all have to take one point which is very
important, not only for Craftsman as a company but for everybody, the commodity price will
show increased top line, we have to knock that off and look at the operational efficiency because
on the commodity price increase even we pass it on to the customer there will be no EBIT
margin. But you're right, if we look at it, the steel portion took up the bulk of the industrial
engineering sector business. Once we price it right, now in the current quarter this will get
corrected.

Management Commentary:
I wish to restrict my answers to the domain where we are present. We are only in ancillary. I
would say that on the farm sector, almost 90% of the major OEMs we are supplying. That is
very clear, that is one thing. Second thing is the OEMs will have a better clue about how the
market is moving, the numbers are moving and their strategies. And for the farm sector by itself
for Craftsman, on the powertrain, it's a smaller portion I would say. I don't think we will get
impacted in a way where its flattish also. We will be having a decent growth because we are
present in the newer models and higher value at models. So, I don't want to get into details that
I do not know practically because the market is changing on a month-to-month basis, we have
to consult OEMs and get back but you may have a better clue when you talk to the OEMs.

Analyst (Rajesh Kothari) asked:
You mentioned that machining revenue and non-machining revenue, what is the breakup in June
‘21? Because you mentioned that it is broadly same and if it is not it will adjust over a period of
time so it was slightly confusing comment. Can you give the actual breakup between machining
revenue and non-machining revenue in June ‘21 this quarter?

Management answered:
I think CFO will take the number out, what I said was sometimes what happens in one month
may change in the next month, so it is not uniform. It depends on customer demand. Just a minute
I will read out the numbers. we have done 113 crores machining in Q1.

Management Commentary:
This year as I speak even in spite of the pandemic our EBITDA and our debt will be more or
less around the same levels. Maybe EBITDA may be higher than the debt where the closing
coming forward. Yes, it'll throw up a lot of cash because of the depreciation and the higher than
the CAPEX what are we doing? We are averse to doing any joint venture; we don't have any
manufacturing joint venture at all. The Carl Stahl Craftsman is only a trading company. Where
a very limited capital has been put and we are selling Craftsman products through the joint
venture because they are a technological support on the marketing and application side. They’re
€300 million a German major family-owned company who has in turn putting their capital and
giving knowledge to us on the market side, not on the manufacturing. We don't have any other
manufacturing joint venture and we don't intend to get into any manufacturing joint venture.
When you mentioned Daimler, it is part of our powertrain business, there are many customers
were invested into special purpose equipment for the manufacturing as part of the sharing of the
CAPEX. So, that will continue that is not a joint venture or something like that to de-risk our
investments, that is what it is and sharing of the investments. Going forward, overseas we have
only two man company, which is a trading company cum sales and service support to our marine
customers, which was started in 2008 and unluckily Lehman crisis hit, we ran into that further,
but last 5 years it is continuously making profit, but we have only 2 employees there is no new
manufacturing activity and the products what we manufacture here are being sold and distributed
to various dealers there. So, there’s no flight of capital from the parent company to any subsidiary
or into any joint venture. I think the other office in Singapore we have wounded down. We are
informed the stock exchange. So, today our consolidated revenue to the standalone revenue will
be hardly 2%-3% difference and the profit also will be 2%-3% higher. It will remain that level.
We don't believe in diluting our management nor the capital into subsidiaries or a joint venture.

Management Commentary:
Yes, that is on the auto aluminium sector which is starting production, this is only in the sampling
stage. The FY23 is the start of production. We've got a very good contract and the peak level of
the business is around 200 crores which we will touch in FY24. With that I think we'll be running
out of capacity on auto aluminium business by the end of FY23 itself. This is a good news
because this is a big foray for us into the passenger vehicle segment, today predominantly we
are in the two-wheeler segment and very less on the passenger vehicle segment and some of the
commercial vehicle segment. Adding to this, I have one important point is on the auto aluminium
business, we had a major breakthrough. We have got some farm equipment products also. This
is the first time in India. I think that larger parts are coming on, these are very large die-casting
machines. We got the orders and the products is under development. We see this trend on an
increasing basis also.

results.question: What was the Free Cash Flow yield (FCF / Market Cap) for Craftsman Automation in FY26?
results.expected_answer: Not computable. First, FCF itself cannot be reliably computed because the dataset has no distinct Capex line item (only aggregate Cash from Investing Activity). Second, Market Cap is not available in the financial dataset. Both components required for this ratio are missing.
results.answer:
## Details

To calculate Free Cash Flow (FCF) and then Free Cash Flow Yield (FCF / Market Cap), we need:

- Free Cash Flow (FCF) = Cash from Operating Activities - Capital Expenditure (Capex)
- Market Capitalization (Market Cap) = Share Price × Number of Shares (or alternatively Equity Share Capital × Market Price per share)

From the provided context:

- Cash from Operating Activity (FY26) = ₹521.68 Cr (from cash_flow sheet)
- Capital Expenditure (Capex) is not explicitly stated in the retrieved context. The balance sheet shows:
  - Net Block = ₹4676.32 Cr
  - Capital Work in Progress (CWIP) = ₹382.33 Cr
  But no explicit Capex figure is given.
- Market Capitalization is not directly provided.
  - Equity Share Capital = ₹11.93 Cr (from balance_sheet)
  - Number of shares or share price is not provided in the retrieved context.

Since Capex is not disclosed explicitly, and Market Cap cannot be computed without share price or number of shares, the Free Cash Flow and Free Cash Flow Yield cannot be calculated.

## Summary

Free Cash Flow yield for Craftsman Automation in FY26 cannot be calculated because the required figures for Capital Expenditure and Market Capitalization (share price or number of shares) are not available in the retrieved context.
